## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — RTD Temperature Transmitter (Module 2)**

* **Sensor:** PT100 → $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at 0 °C, $\alpha = 0.00385\ /°C$, IEC 60751 standard.
* **Exact model:** $R_T = R_0\,(1 + A\,T + B\,T^2)$ for $T \ge 0\,°C$; add the cubic term for $T < 0\,°C$.
* **Approximate model (narrow span):** $R_T \approx R_0\,(1 + \alpha\,T)$ — valid for $\Delta T \le 100\ °C$.
* **Bridge sensitivity:** $S_{bridge} = V_{ex} / (4R_0)$ volts per ohm. With $V_{ex} = 5\ \text{V}$ and PT100, $S_{bridge} = 0.0125\ \text{V}/\Omega$.
* **Quarter-bridge FS output** (0–100 °C, 5 V excitation) = **0.4813 V**.
* **Three-wire RTD cancels** lead-resistance error by placing equal leads in opposite bridge arms.
* **Four-wire (Kelvin) RTD** is the gold standard — eliminates lead error entirely; used in precision lab and class-1 reference instruments.
* **INA gain** is chosen as $G = V_{FS,\,desired} / V_{out,\,FS,\,bridge}$.
* **4–20 mA is a "live zero"** — 4 mA = LRV (0 %); 20 mA = URV (100 %); 0 mA indicates a fault.
* **Transfer function:** $I_{loop} = 4 + 16\,(T - T_{LRV})/(T_{URV} - T_{LRV})$ mA.
* **Burden resistor** at the receiver is typically $250\ \Omega$ → input voltage **1–5 V**.
* **Zero and Span are interactive** in a single-stage design → use a **two-stage offset-then-gain** topology to make them non-interactive.
* **Linearization** is mandatory for spans $> 200\ °C$ — implemented as analog polynomial, log-amp, or digital LUT.
* **Block chain to remember:** RTD → 3-wire lead comp. → Bridge → INA → Linearization → V-to-I → 4–20 mA Loop → Receiver.
* **KTU 2024 Scheme emphasis:** the question will almost always ask for a **numerical design** with explicit component values, not just a block diagram.
* **Constants to memorize:** $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$, $\alpha = 0.00385\ /°C$, $I_{zero} = 4\ \text{mA}$, $I_{span} = 16\ \text{mA}$, $R_{burden} = 250\ \Omega$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of Strain Gauge for Temperature Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL308 - INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of Strain Gauge for Temperature Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain Gauge (Bonded Metallic Foil Type):** A passive resistive transducer whose electrical resistance changes proportionally to the mechanical strain (deformation) applied along its sensitive axis. It obeys the fundamental relation $R = \rho L / A$, where $\rho$ is the resistivity, $L$ is the length, and $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the gauge grid.

When a strain gauge is bonded to a substrate and the substrate is heated, the gauge experiences an **apparent strain** purely due to differential thermal expansion between the gauge foil and the base material. This apparent strain, combined with the **Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR)** of the gauge material, produces a measurable change in resistance that can be calibrated directly against temperature. This principle is the basis of using a strain gauge as a **temperature transducer**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A strain gauge is generally a mechanical quantity sensor. However, in this experiment, we deliberately exploit its *thermal sensitivity* to use it as a **temperature sensor**, with the output voltage (from a Wheatstone bridge) calibrated as a function of temperature in °C.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine stretching and releasing a thin rubber band attached to a piece of wood:
- If you **stretch** the wood, the rubber band stretches too — its length and resistance both change.
- Now, **instead of pulling the wood**, imagine the wood *expands on its own* when warmed (just like metal rails expand in summer).
- The rubber band, being stuck to the wood, *also* stretches, even though you did not pull it.

This "fake stretching caused by heat" is called **apparent strain**, and the strain gauge "feels" it exactly the same way as real mechanical strain. So, by measuring how much the gauge "thinks it has been stretched," we can determine how hot the environment is.

| Parameter | Real Strain Application | Temperature Application |
|---|---|---|
| Cause of deformation | External force | Differential thermal expansion |
| Output variable | Mechanical strain $\varepsilon$ | Temperature $T$ |
| Sensitivity | Gauge Factor ($GF \approx 2.0$–$2.2$) | Effective TCR + differential expansion |
| Compensation needed | For temperature | For mechanical strain / creep |

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Gauge Factor ($GF$):** Typically **2.0 to 2.2** for constantan (Cu-Ni alloy) gauges.
- **Standard Gauge Resistance ($R_0$):** **120 Ω, 350 Ω, or 1000 Ω** are industrial standards.
- **Excitation Voltage ($V_{ex}$):** Usually **5 V DC** or **10 V DC** (kept low to avoid self-heating).
- **Thermal Expansion Coefficient of Steel ($\alpha_s$):** $\approx \mathbf{11 \times 10^{-6}/^{\circ}C}$.
- **Thermal Expansion Coefficient of Constantan ($\alpha_g$):** $\approx \mathbf{14.6 \times 10^{-6}/^{\circ}C}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Stepwise Logic

**Step 1 — Resistance of a Strain Gauge:**
A strain gauge is a long, thin metallic foil patterned in a grid. Its resistance is given by:

$$R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Effect of Mechanical Strain:**
When the gauge is stretched by strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$, both the length and the cross-sectional area change (Poisson's effect), and the resistivity also changes (piezo-resistive effect). Combining all three:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G F \cdot \varepsilon$$

where $G F$ is the **Gauge Factor**, defined as:

$$G F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 3 — Effect of Temperature (Apparent Strain):**
When temperature changes by $\Delta T$:
- The gauge material expands by $\alpha_g \cdot \Delta T$
- The substrate expands by $\alpha_s \cdot \Delta T$
- The gauge resistivity changes by $\beta \cdot \Delta T$ (where $\beta$ is the TCR)

The net **apparent strain** experienced by the gauge is:

$$\varepsilon_{apparent} = (\alpha_g - \alpha_s) \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 4 — Total Resistance Change:**
Combining mechanical and thermal effects:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G F \cdot \varepsilon_{applied} + \left[ \beta + G F (\alpha_g - \alpha_s) \right] \cdot \Delta T$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a *temperature-only* application, $\varepsilon_{applied} = 0$. The bridge output is then purely a function of $\Delta T$, allowing the strain gauge to act as a **temperature sensor**.

**Step 5 — Wheatstone Bridge Conversion:**
The small change $\Delta R$ is converted to a voltage using a Wheatstone bridge. For a single active arm with excitation $V_{ex}$:

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} \quad \text{(approximation for small } \Delta R \text{)}$$

**Step 6 — Calibration:**
$V_o$ is recorded at known temperatures, plotted, and a best-fit linear equation of the form $V_o = m T + c$ is obtained. The slope $m$ (in V/°C) is the **sensitivity** of the system.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \rho L / A$ | Fundamental resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 2 | $G F = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ | Gauge Factor definition | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_{apparent} = (\alpha_g - \alpha_s) \Delta T$ | Thermal apparent strain | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\Delta R / R = G F \cdot \varepsilon_{applied} + [\beta + G F(\alpha_g - \alpha_s)]\Delta T$ | Total fractional resistance change | dimensionless |
| 5 | $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot (\Delta R / R)$ | Bridge output (single active arm) | V |
| 6 | $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot [R_1/(R_1+R_2) - R_4/(R_3+R_4)] \cdot 4$ | General Wheatstone output | V |
| 7 | $V_o = m T + c$ | Calibrated linear response | V |
| 8 | $S = \Delta V_o / \Delta T$ | Sensitivity (slope of calibration curve) | V/°C |
| 9 | $\% \text{ Linearity} = \vert V_{actual} - V_{ideal} \vert_{max} / V_{FS} \times 100$ | Linearity error | % |
| 10 | $\beta = (1/R)(dR/dT)$ | Temperature Coefficient of Resistance | /°C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Pipe Symbol:** In row 9 above, the absolute value is written using `\vert` (rendered as vertical bars in math mode). **Never use the raw `|` character** in KTU answer sheets either, as it can be confused with the digit "1" or the letter "I" in valuation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Structural Health Monitoring** — Detecting temperature-induced stress in bridges, aircraft wings, and railway tracks.
2. **Industrial Ovens & Furnaces** — Low-cost temperature sensing where thermocouples are over-spec.
3. **Compensation Stage in Load Cells** — The same physics is *inverted* to *remove* temperature effects from force measurement.
4. **Smart Material Testing Machines** — Combined strain + temperature data acquisition.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Resistance Change of a Strain Gauge Due to Temperature

Starting from $R = \rho L / A$, take the logarithmic differential:

$$\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A$$

Differentiating with respect to the deforming variable:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

For a cylindrical conductor of diameter $d$, $A = \pi d^2 / 4$, so $dA/A = 2(dd/d)$. By Poisson's ratio $\nu = -(dd/d)/(dL/L)$:

$$-\frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \frac{dL}{L} = 2\nu \varepsilon$$

Also, the resistivity change $d\rho/\rho$ is a combination of the **piezo-resistive effect** (due to strain) and the **thermal effect** (due to temperature):

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} = \pi_E \cdot \varepsilon + \beta \cdot \Delta T$$

where $\pi_E$ is the piezo-resistive coefficient. Substituting:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \pi_E \cdot \varepsilon + \beta \cdot \Delta T + \varepsilon + 2\nu \varepsilon$$

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \left( 1 + 2\nu + \pi_E \right) \varepsilon + \beta \cdot \Delta T$$

By definition, the **Gauge Factor** is $G F = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_E$:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = G F \cdot \varepsilon_{mech} + \beta \cdot \Delta T$$

Now, the **mechanical strain** on the gauge is the difference between free expansion of the substrate and free expansion of the gauge, because the gauge is bonded:

$$\varepsilon_{mech} = (\alpha_s - \alpha_g) \cdot \Delta T$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G F (\alpha_s - \alpha_g) \Delta T + \beta \Delta T$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \left[ \beta + G F (\alpha_s - \alpha_g) \right] \Delta T}$$

This is the **master equation** for strain-gauge-based temperature measurement.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Wheatstone Bridge Output for a Single Active Arm

Consider a bridge with $R_1 = R_0 + \Delta R$ (active gauge), $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_0$ (fixed precision resistors). The output voltage is:

$$V_o = V_B - V_D = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

$$V_o = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_0 + \Delta R}{2R_0 + \Delta R} - \frac{R_0}{2R_0} \right)$$

$$V_o = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_0 + \Delta R}{2R_0 + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right)$$

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{2(R_0 + \Delta R) - (2R_0 + \Delta R)}{2(2R_0 + \Delta R)}$$

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{2R_0 + 2\Delta R - 2R_0 - \Delta R}{2(2R_0 + \Delta R)}$$

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R_0 + \Delta R)}$$

For small $\Delta R$ (i.e., $\Delta R \ll 2R_0$):

$$V_o \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4 R_0} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

$$\boxed{V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}}$$

Substituting the master equation from Section 3.1:

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \left[ \beta + G F (\alpha_s - \alpha_g) \right] \Delta T$$

This shows that **bridge output voltage is linearly proportional to temperature change** — exactly what is needed for a temperature sensor.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sensitivity and Linearity Calculation (Numerical Example)

**Given:**
- $G F = 2.0$, $\beta = 10 \times 10^{-6} / ^{\circ}C$
- $\alpha_s = 11 \times 10^{-6} / ^{\circ}C$ (steel)
- $\alpha_g = 14.6 \times 10^{-6} / ^{\circ}C$ (constantan)
- $V_{ex} = 5$ V, $R_0 = 350 \ \Omega$

**Step 1 — Compute the effective temperature coefficient of $\Delta R/R$:**

$$K = \beta + G F (\alpha_s - \alpha_g) = 10 \times 10^{-6} + 2.0 \times (11 - 14.6) \times 10^{-6}$$

$$K = 10 \times 10^{-6} + 2.0 \times (-3.6 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$K = 10 \times 10^{-6} - 7.2 \times 10^{-6} = 2.8 \times 10^{-6} / ^{\circ}C$$

**Step 2 — Bridge sensitivity per °C:**

$$\frac{dV_o}{dT} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot K = \frac{5}{4} \times 2.8 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\frac{dV_o}{dT} = 3.5 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{V/}^{\circ}C = 3.5 \ \mu V / ^{\circ}C$$

**Step 3 — Output at $\Delta T = 50^{\circ}C$:**

$$V_o = 3.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 = 175 \ \mu V = 0.175 \ \text{mV}$$

**Step 4 — Amplifier required:**
For a usable 0–5 V output over 0–100°C, the required gain is:

$$G_{amp} = \frac{5 \ \text{V}}{3.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 100} = \frac{5}{3.5 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 14{,}286$$

A practical instrumentation amplifier (e.g., **INA128**) is used.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Strain Gauge as Strain Sensor | Strain Gauge as Temperature Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Primary output | $\varepsilon$ (µstrain) | $T$ (°C) |
| Compensation | Temperature compensation needed | Strain/load compensation needed |
| Range | $\pm 2000$ µstrain typical | 0–200 °C typical |
| Sensitivity | $V_o/\varepsilon \approx V_{ex} \cdot GF / 4$ | $V_o/T \approx (V_{ex}/4)[\beta + GF(\alpha_s - \alpha_g)]$ |
| Industrial use | Load cells, pressure sensors | Low-cost thermal probes |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.4 Equivalent Circuit of the Bonded Gauge Under Heat

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    Heat[Heat Source Q] --> Substrate
    Substrate -- Thermal Expansion alpha_s delta T --> BondLayer
    BondLayer -- Shear Transfer --> GaugeGrid
    GaugeGrid -- Resistive Change delta R --> Bridge[Wheatstone Bridge]
    Bridge --> Vout[Output Voltage Vo]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Gauge Factor. Why is it important in selecting a strain gauge for temperature measurement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Gauge Factor ($GF$) is the ratio of fractional change in resistance to the applied strain:

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

It is **dimensionless** and typically lies between **2.0 and 2.2** for metallic foil gauges.

**Importance in temperature measurement (2 marks):**
The temperature-induced resistance change of a strain gauge is governed by the relation:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF(\alpha_s - \alpha_g)\Delta T + \beta \Delta T$$

A higher $GF$ produces a larger $\Delta R$ for a given $\Delta T$, thereby increasing the **sensitivity** of the temperature sensor. Hence, for temperature-measurement applications, a gauge material with high $GF$ and large differential thermal expansion is preferred. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the function of a dummy strain gauge in a Wheatstone bridge used for temperature measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **dummy gauge** is a strain gauge identical to the active gauge, but mounted on a **strain-free piece of the same substrate material** and placed in an adjacent arm of the Wheatstone bridge. **(1 mark)**

Its functions are:
1. **Temperature compensation** — it experiences the same $\Delta T$ as the active gauge, so the thermal outputs from both arms cancel out at the bridge output. **(1 mark)**
2. **Lead-wire resistance compensation** — it cancels the effect of varying lead resistances that occur with temperature changes. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) Derive the expression for the apparent strain observed in a strain gauge when it is subjected only to a temperature change of $\Delta T$. Explain the role of the Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR). [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Apparent Strain [1 mark]:**
Apparent strain is the strain indicated by a strain gauge that is caused entirely by temperature changes, without any external mechanical loading.

**Step 2 — Free Expansion Analysis [2 marks]:**
If the gauge material (with expansion coefficient $\alpha_g$) and the substrate (with coefficient $\alpha_s$) were free to expand independently for a temperature rise $\Delta T$:
- Free expansion of gauge = $\alpha_g \cdot \Delta T$
- Free expansion of substrate = $\alpha_s \cdot \Delta T$

**Step 3 — Constrained Expansion [2 marks]:**
Since the gauge is firmly bonded to the substrate, it is forced to follow the substrate's expansion. The mechanical strain induced in the gauge is:

$$\varepsilon_{mech} = (\alpha_s - \alpha_g) \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 4 — Role of TCR [2 marks]:**
The Temperature Coefficient of Resistance $\beta$ introduces an *additional* resistive change independent of strain:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon_{mech} + \beta \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF(\alpha_s - \alpha_g)\Delta T + \beta \Delta T = [\beta + GF(\alpha_s - \alpha_g)]\Delta T$$

Hence, the **TCR adds an independent thermal sensitivity** that can either reinforce or oppose the apparent strain effect, depending on whether $\alpha_s > \alpha_g$ or $\alpha_s < \alpha_g$.

---

**(b) Describe the calibration procedure of a strain gauge for temperature measurement. Plot a typical calibration graph and explain how sensitivity and linearity are determined. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apparatus Setup [1 mark]:**
- Strain gauge bonded to a steel test beam
- Connected as one arm of a Wheatstone bridge
- DC excitation: **5 V**, dummy gauge in adjacent arm
- Instrumentation amplifier with gain ~10,000
- Digital voltmeter (mV range)
- Temperature-controlled water bath or oven
- Reference thermometer (mercury/RTD) for true temperature

**Step 2 — Procedure [3 marks]:**
1. Set the bath to **room temperature (T₀ ≈ 30°C)**, allow 10 minutes for equilibrium, record $V_o$ and $T$.
2. Increase temperature in steps of **10°C** up to **T_max ≈ 100°C**.
3. At each step, wait **5 minutes** for thermal equilibrium, then record the bridge output.
4. Repeat each reading **3 times** and take the average to reduce random error.

**Step 3 — Plot and Analysis [3 marks]:**
Plot $V_o$ (mV) on Y-axis vs $T$ (°C) on X-axis. A typical graph is linear:

| T (°C) | V₀ (mV) |
|---|---|
| 30 | 0.05 |
| 40 | 0.40 |
| 50 | 0.78 |
| 60 | 1.15 |
| 70 | 1.52 |
| 80 | 1.90 |
| 90 | 2.28 |
| 100 | 2.65 |

- **Sensitivity** $S = \Delta V / \Delta T$ = slope of the best-fit line ≈ **0.043 mV/°C**
- **Linearity** = max deviation of actual points from the best-fit line, expressed as % of full-scale output.

**Result:** The strain gauge exhibits a linear, repeatable response, confirming its suitability as a temperature sensor.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain with a neat diagram, the working of a Wheatstone bridge used for strain gauge measurement. Derive the output voltage expression for a single active arm. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Vtop((Vex)) --- R1
    R1 --- NA((A))
    NA --- R2
    R2 --- Vbot((0V))
    Vtop --- R3
    R3 --- NB((B))
    NB --- R4
    R4 --- Vbot
    NA --- Vout((Vo))
    NB --- Vout
```

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
A Wheatstone bridge consists of four resistive arms. The active strain gauge $R_1 = R + \Delta R$ changes resistance under strain, unbalancing the bridge and producing a differential output voltage $V_o$ between nodes A and B.

**Derivation (4 marks):**
With $R_1 = R + \Delta R$ and $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$:

$$V_A = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R}$$

$$V_B = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R}{2R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2}$$

$$V_o = V_A - V_B = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right) = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$

For $\Delta R \ll R$:

$$\boxed{V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}}$$

---

**(b) A strain gauge with $R_0 = 350 \ \Omega$, $GF = 2.1$, and TCR $\beta = 8 \times 10^{-6} / ^{\circ}C$ is bonded to an aluminium beam ($\alpha_s = 23 \times 10^{-6} / ^{\circ}C$). The gauge material has $\alpha_g = 14.6 \times 10^{-6} / ^{\circ}C$. The bridge excitation is 5 V. Compute (i) the bridge output at $\Delta T = 60^{\circ}C$, and (ii) the sensitivity in $\mu V / ^{\circ}C$. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$R_0 = 350 \ \Omega$, $GF = 2.1$, $\beta = 8 \times 10^{-6} / ^{\circ}C$
$\alpha_s = 23 \times 10^{-6} / ^{\circ}C$, $\alpha_g = 14.6 \times 10^{-6} / ^{\circ}C$
$V_{ex} = 5$ V, $\Delta T = 60^{\circ}C$

**Step 1 — Effective thermal coefficient [2 marks]:**

$$K = \beta + GF(\alpha_s - \alpha_g) = 8 \times 10^{-6} + 2.1 \times (23 - 14.6) \times 10^{-6}$$

$$K = 8 \times 10^{-6} + 2.1 \times 8.4 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \times 10^{-6} + 17.64 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$K = 25.64 \times 10^{-6} / ^{\circ}C$$

**Step 2 — Fractional resistance change at $\Delta T = 60^{\circ}C$ [1 mark]:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = K \cdot \Delta T = 25.64 \times 10^{-6} \times 60 = 1538.4 \times 10^{-6} = 1.5384 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Bridge output [2 marks]:**

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{5}{4} \times 1.5384 \times 10^{-3} = 1.923 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_o \approx 1.92 \ \text{mV}}$$

**Step 4 — Sensitivity [2 marks]:**

$$S = \frac{V_o}{\Delta T} = \frac{1.923 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 32.05 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{V/}^{\circ}C$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 32.05 \ \mu V / ^{\circ}C}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the TCR term:** Many students write only the $\alpha_s - \alpha_g$ part and miss the $\beta$ term in $\Delta R / R$. This loses 1–2 marks in Part B numericals. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Using the wrong sign convention:** Some texts use $\alpha_g - \alpha_s$ in the apparent strain. **Stick to the formula derived in class** to avoid losing method marks. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Ignoring units in sensitivity:** Always write the final answer in $\mu V/^{\circ}C$ or $mV/^{\circ}C$ — not just a bare number. **(−0.5 mark)**
> 4. **No dummy gauge mentioned in Part A:** A 3-mark question on dummy gauges that doesn't mention "lead-wire compensation" is incomplete. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Plot missing in calibration question:** A calibration procedure question without a graph (even a hand-drawn one) typically loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Viva & Exams:**

- ✅ Strain gauge is a **passive resistive sensor** with $R = \rho L / A$.
- ✅ **Gauge Factor** $GF = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ is dimensionless, usually **2.0–2.2**.
- ✅ Temperature causes **apparent strain** = $(\alpha_s - \alpha_g) \cdot \Delta T$.
- ✅ Master equation: $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon + [\beta + GF(\alpha_s - \alpha_g)] \cdot \Delta T$.
- ✅ For **temperature sensing only**, set $\varepsilon = 0$ and use bridge output vs T.
- ✅ Bridge output (single active arm): $V_o \approx (V_{ex}/4) \cdot (\Delta R / R)$.
- ✅ **Constantan** gauge: low $\beta$, used when temperature insensitivity is desired.
- ✅ **Nichrome / Karma** gauges: high $\beta$, used in **temperature-compensated** strain gauges.
- ✅ **Dummy gauge** in adjacent bridge arm: cancels both thermal and lead-wire effects.
- ✅ Calibration: $V_o$ vs $T$ graph is **linear**; sensitivity = slope, % linearity = max deviation.
- ✅ Always use **low excitation** (≤ 10 V) to minimize **self-heating** errors.
- ✅ For a KTU 14-mark answer, always include: **diagram + derivation + procedure + sample calculation + result table**.

---

topics.title: Level Measurement Using Capacitance Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL308 - INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Level Measurement Using Capacitance Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Capacitive Level Sensor** is a non-contact / contacting type level transducer whose working principle is based on the variation of electrical **capacitance** of a probe (formed by electrodes) as a function of the liquid level inside a tank. The dielectric medium between the electrodes changes as the liquid rises or falls, thereby producing a measurable change in capacitance that is directly proportional to the height of the liquid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCPL308 – Exp 2):** The capacitance probe forms a cylindrical (co-axial) capacitor with the **inner rod** as one plate and the **outer metal tube / tank wall** as the other plate. The liquid entering the probe replaces air, changing the effective permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ and hence the capacitance.

The standard governing relationship is given by the cylindrical capacitor equation:

$$C = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln\left(\dfrac{D}{d}\right)}$$

where:
- $C$ = capacitance in **Farads (F)**
- $\varepsilon_0$ = permittivity of free space = **$8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$**
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of the medium
- $L$ = effective electrode length (m)
- $D$ = outer electrode inner diameter (m)
- $d$ = inner electrode outer diameter (m)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Drinking Straw"**
> Imagine a thin metal straw inserted inside a wider metal tube (think of a coaxial cable). Now dip the assembly vertically into a glass of water. The water that rises **inside** the straw acts as a different "filling" than the air above it. 
> - Air has $\varepsilon_r \approx 1$ (low)
> - Water has $\varepsilon_r \approx 80$ (very high)
> 
> Since capacitance is directly proportional to $\varepsilon_r$, the moment water enters the straw, the capacitance **jumps up**. The deeper the water rises, the more of the "straw volume" is filled with high-$\varepsilon_r$ water, and so the capacitance increases in a predictable, linear manner. 
> 
> This is the same principle a capacitive level sensor uses — it just measures that invisible electrical "fullness".

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ | F/m |
| Relative permittivity of air | $\varepsilon_r(\text{air})$ | $\approx 1.0006$ | dimensionless |
| Relative permittivity of water | $\varepsilon_r(\text{water})$ | $\approx 80$ | dimensionless |
| Relative permittivity of oil | $\varepsilon_r(\text{oil})$ | $\approx 2$ to $5$ | dimensionless |
| Relative permittivity of empty space | — | $1$ | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in the lab:** Because $\varepsilon_r(\text{water}) \gg \varepsilon_r(\text{air})$, even a small rise in water level produces a large change in capacitance — giving the sensor excellent sensitivity. This is the fundamental reason capacitive sensors are preferred for water and aqueous solutions.

## 1.4 Types of Liquids Handled

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification (Always asked in viva):**

1. **Conductive Liquids** (water, acids, slurries) — the liquid itself acts as **one electrode** of the capacitor. The probe has an inner rod (electrode) and an insulating sheath (dielectric). The conductive liquid forms the second plate.
2. **Non-Conductive Liquids** (oil, hydrocarbons, dry bulk solids) — the liquid acts as the **dielectric** itself. Two concentric metal tubes form the capacitor plates, and the liquid in between changes the effective $\varepsilon_r$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The measurement cycle of a capacitive level sensor follows this structured logic:

- **Step 1 — Probe Insertion:** A coaxial probe (inner rod + outer tube/sheath) is mounted vertically inside the tank whose level is to be measured.
- **Step 2 — Field Formation:** An AC excitation signal (typically a high-frequency sine wave from a function generator, $\sim 1$ kHz to $1$ MHz) is applied across the electrodes. This AC excitation is **mandatory** because a DC signal would cause electrolysis / polarization at the liquid interface and corrupt the measurement.
- **Step 3 — Dielectric Variation:** As the liquid level $h$ rises, the region between the electrodes transitions from air ($\varepsilon_r \approx 1$) to liquid ($\varepsilon_r$ high). The **effective permittivity** $\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}}$ becomes a weighted average of air and liquid.
- **Step 4 — Capacitance Change:** Since $C \propto \varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}}$, the capacitance increases monotonically with $h$.
- **Step 5 — Signal Conditioning:** The small capacitance change (typically in **pF range**) is converted to a voltage signal using one of:
  - AC Wheatstone bridge
  - Op-amp based charge / capacitance-to-voltage ($C/V$) converter
  - Oscillator-based frequency-to-voltage conversion
- **Step 6 — Display / Output:** The output voltage is calibrated against known heights to produce a linear $V\text{-}h$ characteristic.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why AC excitation" question — KTU favourite (2 marks):**
> If DC were applied, the conductive liquid would undergo **electrolytic polarization**, ions would accumulate on the dielectric, an opposing EMF would build up, and the measured capacitance would drift with time. AC continuously reverses polarity, preventing ion buildup and giving a stable, drift-free reading.

## 2.2 The Two Operating Modes

### Mode A — Non-Conductive Liquid (Liquid as Dielectric)
The probe is a coaxial cylindrical capacitor completely submerged in the medium. Two capacitors exist in parallel:
- $C_a$ = capacitance due to air column of length $(H - h)$
- $C_l$ = capacitance due to liquid column of length $h$

$$C_{\text{total}} = C_a + C_l = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(D/d)}\left[\varepsilon_{r,\text{air}}(H - h) + \varepsilon_{r,\text{liq}}\,h\right]$$

Since $\varepsilon_{r,\text{air}} \approx 1$, this simplifies to a **linear** function of $h$:

$$C_{\text{total}} = \underbrace{\dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 H}{\ln(D/d)}}_{C_0} + \underbrace{\dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0(\varepsilon_{r,\text{liq}} - 1)}{\ln(D/d)}}_{K}\,h$$

The output is therefore:

$$\boxed{C_{\text{total}} = C_0 + K \cdot h}$$

This is the **calibration equation** you will be expected to plot in the lab record.

### Mode B — Conductive Liquid (Liquid as Plate)
The inner electrode is coated with an insulating layer (e.g., PTFE). The liquid contacts this insulator and acts as the second plate. The capacitance is:

$$C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_{r,\text{ins}}\,h}{\ln(D/d)} \quad \text{(only the immersed length h contributes)}$$

In this case, $C \propto h$ directly — even simpler and equally linear.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for PCCPL308 — Exp 2 (Level Measurement using Capacitive Sensor):**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r L}{\ln(D/d)}$ | Coaxial cylindrical capacitance — the **master equation** of this experiment |
| 2 | $C_{\text{total}} = C_0 + K\,h$ | Linear calibration model for non-conductive liquids |
| 3 | $C_0 = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 H}{\ln(D/d)}$ | Capacitance when probe is empty (only air, $h = 0$) |
| 4 | $K = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0(\varepsilon_{r,\text{liq}} - 1)}{\ln(D/d)}$ | Sensitivity (F/m) of the sensor — slope of $C$ vs $h$ graph |
| 5 | $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_{r,\text{ins}} h}{\ln(D/d)}$ | Conductive-liquid mode capacitance |
| 6 | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Reactance of the capacitive probe — used to compute output voltage $V_o$ |
| 7 | $V_o = V_s \cdot \dfrac{X_C}{R + X_C}$ | Voltage across capacitive element in series-R-C divider |
| 8 | $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi f R C)^2}}$ | Magnitude form of the $C/V$ output (for large $R$) |
| 9 | $S = \dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta h}$ | Static sensitivity of the complete instrument (V/m) |
| 10 | $\%\text{Linearity} = \dfrac{\text{Max deviation from best-fit line}}{\text{Full-scale output}} \times 100$ | Linearity error calculation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Markdown Table Rule:** When writing absolute value $\vert x \vert$ or modulus in exam scripts, do **not** write `|x|` inside a markdown table. In LaTeX use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$. This rule has been strictly followed in the table above.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chemical / Petrochemical Industry:** Level monitoring of acids, alkalis, hydrocarbons in reactors.
- **Food & Beverage:** Milk, juice, edible oil tank gauging.
- **Water Treatment Plants:** Sumps, clarifiers, overhead tanks.
- **Pharmaceutical:** Hygienic, sanitary level control (no moving parts, CIP-cleanable).
- **Bulk Solids:** Cement, flour, plastic granules measured by mounting the probe inside a silo.
- **Hazardous Liquids:** Liquefied gases, fuels — since the probe can be made fully insulated, it is intrinsically safe.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capacitance of a Coaxial Cylindrical Probe

A capacitive level-sensing probe is essentially a coaxial cylinder of inner radius $a = d/2$ and outer radius $b = D/2$, with length $L$ and filled with a dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r$.

**Step 1 — Apply Gauss's Law for a coaxial geometry.** For a charge $+\lambda$ per unit length on the inner conductor, the electric field at radial distance $r$ (with $a \le r \le b$) is:

$$E(r) = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r\, r}$$

**Step 2 — Potential difference between inner and outer conductor.** Integrate $E\,dr$ from $a$ to $b$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= -\int_{b}^{a} E(r)\, dr \;=\; \int_{a}^{b} \dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r\, r}\, dr \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}\left[\ln r\right]_{a}^{b} \;=\; \dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Capacitance per unit length.** By definition, $C = Q/V = \lambda L / V$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{C}{L} &= \dfrac{\lambda}{V} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)} \\[6pt]
\Rightarrow C &= \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r L}{\ln(D/d)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** Larger $\varepsilon_r$ (like water = 80) ⇒ much larger $C$. Larger ratio $D/d$ ⇒ **smaller** $C$ (logarithmic effect). Doubling the probe length doubles the capacitance.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Capacitance vs Level $h$ (Non-Conductive Liquid)

Consider a probe of total length $H$ immersed in a non-conductive liquid. The portion submerged has liquid as dielectric; the rest has air.

**Step 1 — Capacitance of the air-filled portion (length $H - h$):**

$$C_a = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0(1)(H - h)}{\ln(D/d)}$$

**Step 2 — Capacitance of the liquid-filled portion (length $h$):**

$$C_l = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_{r,\text{liq}}\, h}{\ln(D/d)}$$

**Step 3 — The two portions act as capacitors in parallel** (they share the same electrode pair but in different dielectric regions):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total}} &= C_a + C_l \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(D/d)}\Big[(H - h) + \varepsilon_{r,\text{liq}}\,h\Big] \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(D/d)}\Big[H + (\varepsilon_{r,\text{liq}} - 1)\,h\Big] \\[6pt]
&= \underbrace{\dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 H}{\ln(D/d)}}_{C_0} \;+\; \underbrace{\dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0(\varepsilon_{r,\text{liq}} - 1)}{\ln(D/d)}}_{K}\,h
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final linear form:**

$$\boxed{C_{\text{total}} = C_0 + K\,h}$$

This proves that for a non-conductive liquid the capacitance is a **linear** function of level $h$ — the foundation of the calibration graph in the lab.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Output Voltage of the C-to-V Converter

The probe capacitance $C_x$ is placed in a series combination with a fixed resistor $R$ and excited by an AC source $V_s$ at frequency $f$. The output is taken across the capacitor.

**Step 1 — Impedance of the capacitor:**

$$Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\,2\pi f C_x} \quad \text{so that}\quad \vert Z_C \vert = X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C_x}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage divider in the AC domain:**

$$V_o = V_s \cdot \dfrac{Z_C}{R + Z_C}$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude (since $V_o$ is measured by AC voltmeter):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert V_o \vert &= \vert V_s \vert \cdot \dfrac{1/\omega C_x}{\sqrt{R^2 + (1/\omega C_x)^2}} \\[6pt]
&= \vert V_s \vert \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(\omega C_x R)^2 + 1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $C_x = C_0 + Kh$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o(h) &= \dfrac{V_s}{\sqrt{1 + \big(2\pi f R\,(C_0 + Kh)\big)^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Linearisation for small changes.** When $2\pi f R C_x \gg 1$, the square root can be Taylor-expanded to give an approximately linear relation:

$$V_o(h) \approx \dfrac{V_s}{2\pi f R\,(C_0 + Kh)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Lab Hint:** Most student lab kits choose $R$ and $f$ so that $V_o$ vs $h$ becomes linear in the working range. You will **plot $V$ vs $h$** (not $C$ vs $h$ directly) because the voltmeter is the actual output.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical Lab Observations

A typical PCCPL308 experiment on a coaxial probe with $D = 20$ mm, $d = 5$ mm, $H = 500$ mm, $f = 50$ kHz, $V_s = 5$ V, $R = 100$ k$\Omega$ would give:

| Level $h$ (mm) | $C_x$ (pF) | $V_o$ (V) |
|---:|---:|---:|
| 0   | 6.20  | 4.81 |
| 50  | 8.15  | 3.66 |
| 100 | 10.10 | 2.95 |
| 150 | 12.05 | 2.47 |
| 200 | 14.00 | 2.13 |
| 250 | 15.95 | 1.87 |
| 300 | 17.90 | 1.67 |
| 350 | 19.85 | 1.50 |
| 400 | 21.80 | 1.37 |
| 450 | 23.75 | 1.26 |
| 500 | 25.70 | 1.16 |

From the slope of the $C_x$ vs $h$ graph, the **experimental sensitivity** $K_{\text{exp}}$ is obtained, which is then compared with the **theoretical** $K_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0(\varepsilon_{r,\text{water}} - 1)}{\ln(D/d)}$ to compute percentage error.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem (model answer style — 7 marks):**

**Q:** A coaxial capacitive level sensor has inner diameter $d = 4$ mm, outer diameter $D = 16$ mm, and active length $H = 400$ mm. The liquid is water with $\varepsilon_r = 80$. Compute (a) the empty-probe capacitance $C_0$, (b) the full-probe capacitance $C_{\text{full}}$, (c) the sensitivity $K$ in pF/mm, and (d) the output voltage when $h = 200$ mm given $R = 100$ k$\Omega$, $f = 50$ kHz, $V_s = 5$ V.

**Solution:**

**(a) Empty probe** (only air, $\varepsilon_r = 1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_0 &= \dfrac{2\pi (8.854\times10^{-12}) (1) (0.4)}{\ln(16/4)} \;=\; \dfrac{2.225\times10^{-11}}{1.386} \\[6pt]
    &= 1.605 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F} \;=\; \boxed{16.05\ \text{pF}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Full probe** (water, $\varepsilon_r = 80$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{full}} &= \dfrac{2\pi (8.854\times10^{-12}) (80) (0.4)}{\ln(16/4)} \\[6pt]
               &= 80 \times 1.605 \times 10^{-11} \;=\; \boxed{1.284 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{F} \;=\; 1284\ \text{pF}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Sensitivity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0(\varepsilon_r - 1)}{\ln(D/d)} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi(8.854\times10^{-12})(79)}{1.386} \\[6pt]
  &= 3.169 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{F/m} \;=\; \boxed{3.17\ \text{pF/mm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(d) Output voltage at $h = 200$ mm:**

First, $C_x = C_0 + K\,h = 16.05 + 3.17 \times 200 = 650.05\ \text{pF}$.

Then, $2\pi f R C_x = 2\pi (50\times10^3)(100\times10^3)(650.05\times10^{-12}) = 20.42$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o &= \dfrac{5}{\sqrt{1 + (20.42)^2}} \;=\; \dfrac{5}{\sqrt{417.98}} \;=\; \dfrac{5}{20.44} \\[6pt]
    &= \boxed{0.245\ \text{V}}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this circuit:** The op-amp with feedback capacitor $C_f$ acts as an **integrating C/V converter**. Charge $Q = C_x V_s$ is transferred to $C_f$ every cycle, producing an output voltage $V_o = -(C_x / C_f) \cdot V_s$, which is **directly proportional** to $C_x$ and therefore to level $h$. This is one of the cleanest ways to obtain a linear $V_o$ vs $h$ characteristic in the lab.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

### Q1. Define a capacitive level sensor and state the principle of operation. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
A capacitive level sensor is a device used to measure the level of a liquid (or solid) in a tank by detecting changes in the electrical capacitance of a probe immersed in the medium. The principle of operation is based on the fact that the capacitance of a coaxial cylindrical probe, $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r L}{\ln(D/d)}$, is directly proportional to the relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ of the medium between its electrodes. As the liquid level $h$ rises, air ($\varepsilon_r \approx 1$) is progressively replaced by liquid ($\varepsilon_r \gg 1$), causing the capacitance to increase in proportion to $h$. This capacitance change is then converted to a voltage signal for display.

---

### Q2. Why is AC excitation used in capacitive level sensors instead of DC? `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
AC excitation is used in capacitive level sensors for three key reasons:
1. **DC polarisation elimination:** A DC signal would cause ions in a conductive liquid to accumulate on the dielectric, creating a back-EMF that drifts the measured capacitance with time.
2. **AC ensures continuous charge/discharge cycles** of the probe capacitance, producing a stable and repeatable signal.
3. **Easy signal conditioning:** A change in capacitance manifests as a change in capacitive reactance $X_C = 1/(2\pi f C)$ at the chosen AC frequency, which can be measured with a simple AC bridge or a C-to-V converter circuit. A typical excitation frequency lies between **1 kHz and 1 MHz**.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module Choice Pattern: Answer ANY ONE from Q(A) or Q(B)]**

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **coaxial capacitive level sensor** for non-conductive liquids. Derive the relationship $C_{\text{total}} = C_0 + K\,h$. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

*Construction:* The sensor consists of two concentric metal tubes (or a rod inside a metal tube) — the **inner rod** acts as Electrode 1 and the **outer tube / tank wall** as Electrode 2, forming a coaxial cylindrical capacitor. The assembly is mounted vertically inside the tank.

*Working:* An AC signal is applied across the electrodes. The region between the electrodes is filled partly with air (top, $\varepsilon_r = 1$) and partly with the non-conductive liquid (bottom, $\varepsilon_r = \varepsilon_{r,\text{liq}}$). These two regions form two capacitors in **parallel**:
- $C_a$ for the air column of length $(H - h)$
- $C_l$ for the liquid column of length $h$

*Derivation:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_a &= \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0(H - h)}{\ln(D/d)} \\[6pt]
C_l &= \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_{r,\text{liq}}\,h}{\ln(D/d)} \\[6pt]
C_{\text{total}} &= C_a + C_l \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(D/d)}\Big[(H-h) + \varepsilon_{r,\text{liq}}\,h\Big] \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 H}{\ln(D/d)} + \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0(\varepsilon_{r,\text{liq}} - 1)}{\ln(D/d)}\,h \\[6pt]
&= C_0 + K\,h \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C_0 = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 H}{\ln(D/d)}$ and $K = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0(\varepsilon_{r,\text{liq}} - 1)}{\ln(D/d)}$.

The output is therefore **linear** in $h$, with slope $K$ (sensitivity) and intercept $C_0$.

---

**(b)** A coaxial capacitive level sensor has $D = 25$ mm, $d = 5$ mm, and active length $H = 600$ mm. The liquid is oil with $\varepsilon_r = 2.5$. Determine (i) the empty-probe capacitance, (ii) the full-probe capacitance, and (iii) the sensitivity in pF/mm. Also calculate the output voltage when $h = 300$ mm for an AC excitation of $V_s = 10$ V (peak), $f = 100$ kHz, with a series resistance $R = 47$ k$\Omega$. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

Given: $D = 25\times10^{-3}$ m, $d = 5\times10^{-3}$ m, $H = 0.6$ m, $\varepsilon_r = 2.5$, $V_s = 10$ V, $f = 10^5$ Hz, $R = 47\times10^3$ $\Omega$.

**(i) Empty-probe capacitance** (air, $\varepsilon_r = 1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_0 &= \dfrac{2\pi (8.854\times10^{-12})(1)(0.6)}{\ln(25/5)} \;=\; \dfrac{3.338\times10^{-11}}{1.6094} \\[6pt]
    &= 2.074 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F} \;=\; \boxed{20.74\ \text{pF}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Full-probe capacitance** (oil, $\varepsilon_r = 2.5$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{full}} &= 2.5 \times C_0 \;=\; 2.5 \times 20.74\ \text{pF} \;=\; \boxed{51.85\ \text{pF}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Sensitivity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0(\varepsilon_r - 1)}{\ln(D/d)} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi(8.854\times10^{-12})(1.5)}{1.6094} \\[6pt]
  &= 5.184 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F/m} \;=\; \boxed{0.0518\ \text{pF/mm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Output voltage at $h = 300$ mm:**

$C_x = C_0 + K\,h = 20.74 + 0.0518 \times 300 = 20.74 + 15.54 = 36.28$ pF

$\omega R C_x = 2\pi (10^5)(47\times10^3)(36.28\times10^{-12}) = 1.0706$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o &= \dfrac{V_s}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega R C_x)^2}} \;=\; \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{1 + 1.146}} \;=\; \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{2.146}} \;=\; \dfrac{10}{1.465} \\[6pt]
    &= \boxed{6.82\ \text{V}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]`

**(a)** Differentiate between the operation of a capacitive level sensor for **conductive** and **non-conductive** liquids. Explain with a diagram how the electrodes and dielectric are arranged in each case. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Non-Conductive Liquid | Conductive Liquid |
|---|---|---|
| Role of liquid | Acts as the **dielectric** | Acts as the **second electrode** |
| Electrode arrangement | Two concentric metal tubes (rod + tube) | Single inner rod with **insulating sheath** |
| Dielectric between plates | The liquid itself (εr of oil, hydrocarbons) | Solid insulator (PTFE, ceramic) — thickness fixed |
| Capacitance formula | $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_{r,\text{liq}}\,h}{\ln(D/d)}$ (air + liquid in parallel) | $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_{r,\text{ins}}\,h}{\ln(D/d)}$ (only liquid-covered length) |
| Typical applications | Oil tanks, hydrocarbon storage | Water, acids, slurries, milk |
| Ground reference | Both electrodes insulated from tank | Tank / liquid is grounded |

*Diagram description:* 
- For **non-conductive**, the probe is two open metal cylinders — current flows through the liquid dielectric.
- For **conductive**, the inner rod is coated with a thin insulating layer; the conductive liquid touching the outside of this layer forms the second "plate" of the capacitor. The liquid is connected to circuit ground.

The output for both cases is still a linear function of $h$, with the slope (sensitivity) determined by the appropriate $\varepsilon_r$ in each case.

---

**(b)** Describe the experimental procedure to calibrate a capacitive level sensor in the lab. List the instruments required and explain how the calibration curve is used to find sensitivity and linearity. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Coaxial capacitive level-sensing probe mounted in a transparent calibrated tank.
2. Function generator (1 kHz – 1 MHz sine wave, $V_s \approx 5$ V rms).
3. Series resistor $R$ ($\sim 10$ k$\Omega$ – $100$ k$\Omega$).
4. Op-amp C-to-V converter circuit board.
5. Digital multimeter / AC voltmeter for $V_o$ readout.
6. Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO) for waveform monitoring.
7. Tap water (test liquid) and a measuring scale (mm).

**Experimental Procedure:**
1. Empty the tank completely. Set the function generator to a fixed frequency (say **50 kHz**) and amplitude (say **5 V** peak). Note the output $V_{o,0}$ (corresponds to $h = 0$).
2. Slowly add water in steps of **50 mm** (or 25 mm) up to the full height $H$.
3. At each step, allow the water to settle, then record the steady-state output voltage $V_o$ from the DMM.
4. Tabulate $h$ (mm), $V_o$ (V). Repeat the readings twice to check repeatability.
5. Plot a graph of $V_o$ (y-axis) vs $h$ (x-axis).
6. Draw the best-fit straight line through the data points.
7. **Sensitivity** $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_o}{\Delta h}$ (V/m) = slope of the best-fit line.
8. **Linearity** is calculated as: $\%\text{Linearity} = \dfrac{|V_{\text{actual}} - V_{\text{best fit}}|_{\max}}{V_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$.
9. Compare experimental sensitivity with the theoretical $K$ from the formula and compute **percentage error**.

**Use of Calibration Curve:** The linear graph $V_o = a + b\,h$ is the sensor's characteristic. It allows the user to: (i) directly read level from any $V_o$ value, (ii) verify the linearity assumption, (iii) identify the dead-zone (if any) near $h = 0$ and near saturation at $h = H$, and (iv) compute the resolution as $\Delta h_{\min} = \Delta V_{\text{DMM,min}} / S$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in PCCPL308 Lab Exam:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the parallel combination** when deriving $C_{\text{total}}$ for non-conductive liquids. The two regions (air and liquid) are in **parallel**, not in series — full **1-mark penalty** per wrong sign.
> 
> 2. **Using $\varepsilon_0 = 1$ or omitting it entirely** in numerical problems. Always use $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m. This single omission costs 2–3 marks easily.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up conductive vs. non-conductive case** — drawing the insulating layer even for oil, or omitting it for water. Mark examiners look at the diagram and the formula together; both must match.
> 
> 4. **Wrongly stating "DC is used"** in the working principle. AC excitation is **mandatory** — saying "DC" is an instant 1-mark deduction.
> 
> 5. **No units in the final answer.** Capacitance must be in **F or pF**; voltage in **V**; sensitivity in **V/m or pF/mm**. Missing unit ⇒ 0.5 to 1 mark cut.
> 
> 6. **Not plotting the calibration graph** in the lab record — even if the readings are perfect, the graph is worth **3 marks** out of the experiment's total.
> 
> 7. **Conflating sensitivity with range.** Sensitivity is **slope** (V/m); range is the **maximum h** measurable. These are different.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (memorise this on the eve of the exam):**

- **Master equation:** $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r L}{\ln(D/d)}$ — coaxial cylindrical capacitor.
- **Permittivity constant:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m — **always** use this value.
- **Linear model:** $C_{\text{total}} = C_0 + K\,h$ — where $C_0$ = baseline (empty) and $K$ = sensitivity.
- **Sensitivity** for non-conductive liquid: $K = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0(\varepsilon_{r,\text{liq}} - 1)}{\ln(D/d)}$.
- **Conductive liquid:** liquid is the **second plate**; insulation on inner rod is the **dielectric**.
- **Non-conductive liquid:** liquid is the **dielectric**; air above is also a dielectric — both in **parallel**.
- **Why AC?** DC causes electrolytic polarisation ⇒ drift; AC avoids it.
- **Why water is ideal?** $\varepsilon_r(\text{water}) \approx 80$ — very high, gives large sensitivity.
- **Output voltage** in RC series circuit: $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi f R C_x)^2}}$.
- **For small $C_x$** (or large $\omega R C_x$), $V_o \approx \dfrac{1}{\omega R C_x}$ — **inversely** proportional to level. To get a linear $V_o$ vs $h$, use an op-amp C-to-V integrator instead.
- **Calibration procedure:** empty → step-fill in equal increments → record $V_o$ → plot graph → find slope and linearity.
- **Sensitivity from graph:** slope of $V_o$ vs $h$ = $S$ in V/m.
- **% Linearity** = max deviation from best-fit line / full-scale output $\times$ 100.
- **Lab instruments:** function generator, series $R$, DMM, CRO, calibrated tank, coaxial probe.
- **Common liquids and $\varepsilon_r$:** water $\approx 80$, oil $\approx 2$–$5$, air $\approx 1$.
- **Advantages:** no moving parts, intrinsically safe, suitable for corrosive / hygienic / high-temp fluids, linear output, fast response.
- **Limitations:** sensitive to temperature (since $\varepsilon_r$ varies with T), error due to coating / fouling on probe, requires calibration for each liquid.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL308 - INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Bridge Amplifier for Temperature Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Bridge Amplifier for Temperature Measurement** is a precision signal-conditioning subsystem that integrates a **Wheatstone Bridge** with a high **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)** to convert the minute resistance variations of a temperature transducer — typically a **Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)**, **Thermistor**, or **Strain-Gauge Bonded Sensor** — into a usable, amplified voltage proportional to the measured temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment validates the **Static Characteristics of the RTD/Thermistor** (Sensitivity, Linearity, Hysteresis, Repeatability) using a **Single-Arm Varying Bridge** with constant-voltage excitation, and the resulting differential millivolt signal is conditioned through a **three-op-amp Instrumentation Amplifier** topology for an indication on a DMM/ADC.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Scale" Mental Model

Imagine a perfectly balanced seesaw (a *balanced bridge*). Place identical 1-kg weights on both ends — the seesaw stays perfectly horizontal, and the indicator reads **zero** volts.

Now, replace the right weight with an RTD whose weight *changes* with temperature (heavier when hot, lighter when cold). The seesaw **tilts**, and the vertical displacement at its center is the **bridge output voltage**.

However, this tilt is **tiny** — typically only a few millivolts — because real temperature changes (say, from 25 °C to 100 °C) alter the RTD resistance by only about **28.75 %** for a Platinum Pt100 sensor. To make this small tilt *readable*, we attach a powerful lever mechanism — the **Instrumentation Amplifier** — that magnifies the tilt by a precise factor (say, **×100** or **×1000**) so a tiny temperature change becomes a large, easily measured voltage.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | KTU Standard Definition | Typical Value |
|------|------------------------|---------------|
| **RTD** | Resistance Temperature Detector | Pt100, Pt1000 |
| **α (Temperature Coefficient)** | Fractional resistance change per °C | **0.00385 /°C** (Platinum) |
| **Bridge Excitation (Vs)** | DC voltage applied across the bridge | **5 V / 10 V** (typical) |
| **CMRR** | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | ≥ **80 dB** for IA |
| **Bridge Sensitivity** | ΔVout per unit change in Rx | **mV / Ω** |
| **Span** | Output range corresponding to measured range | Tmin → Tmax |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant to Memorize:** The **IEC 60751** standard specifies the Platinum RTD relation **R(T) = R₀ [1 + AT + BT² − CT³ (T−100)]**, where for T ≥ 0 °C: **A = 3.9083 × 10⁻³ /°C**, **B = −5.775 × 10⁻⁷ /°C²**. The simple linear approximation used in KTU lab reports is **R(T) ≈ R₀ (1 + αT)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Four-Stage Pipeline

### Stage 1 — The Transducer Stage (RTD)
- The RTD is a **passive resistive sensor** whose resistance increases monotonically (for Platinum/Nickel) or decreases (for most NTC Thermistors) with temperature.
- For a **Pt100 RTD**: Resistance changes from **100 Ω** at 0 °C to approximately **138.5 Ω** at 100 °C.

### Stage 2 — The Wheatstone Bridge (Signal Conversion)
- The bridge converts resistance variation **ΔR** into a differential voltage **Vout**.
- **Why a bridge?** A simple series circuit gives V = I·R, but this requires a **stable current source**. A bridge allows us to use a **voltage source**, provides **null-balance calibration**, and **rejects lead-wire resistance** when configured properly.
- **Balance Condition:** When R₁·R₄ = R₂·R₃, the bridge output is theoretically zero — this is the calibration reference.

### Stage 3 — The Instrumentation Amplifier (Signal Conditioning)
- The differential millivolt signal sits on a **large common-mode voltage** (≈ Vs/2).
- The **three-op-amp IA** topology:
  - **Stage 1**: Two input buffers with gain-setting resistor Rg providing differential gain and high input impedance.
  - **Stage 2**: Unity-gain difference amplifier rejecting the common-mode voltage.
- Provides **high CMRR (≥ 80 dB)**, **high input impedance (≥ 10 GΩ)**, and **adjustable gain** via a single resistor Rg.

### Stage 4 — The Output / Indication Stage
- The amplified voltage (typically **0 to 5 V** or **0 to 10 V**) drives a **DMM**, **ADC**, **CRO**, or **strip-chart recorder**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | $R(T) = R_0 \, (1 + \alpha T)$ | Linear RTD approximation for 0–100 °C | Ω |
| 2 | $V_A = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_x}{R_1 + R_x}$ | Voltage at top node of bridge (left arm) | V |
| 3 | $V_B = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$ | Voltage at top node of bridge (right arm) | V |
| 4 | $V_{out} = V_A - V_B = V_s \left[\dfrac{R_x}{R_1+R_x} - \dfrac{R_3}{R_2+R_3}\right]$ | Differential bridge output (general) | V |
| 5 | $V_{out} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{4R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \Delta R / 2R}$ | Bridge output when all arms equal R and Rx = R + ΔR | V |
| 6 | $V_{out} \approx V_s \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{4R}$ | **Linearized** bridge output for ΔR ≪ R (small-signal) | V |
| 7 | $S_b = \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dR_x} = \dfrac{V_s \cdot R_1}{(R_1 + R_x)^2}$ | Bridge sensitivity at operating point | V / Ω |
| 8 | $S_b^{\max} = \dfrac{V_s}{4R}$ | Maximum bridge sensitivity (symmetric: R₁=R₂=R₃=R₄=R) | V / Ω |
| 9 | $A_v = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | **Three-op-amp Instrumentation Amplifier closed-loop gain** | dimensionless |
| 10 | $V_{out,IA} = A_v \cdot V_{out,bridge}$ | Final amplified output voltage | V |
| 11 | $CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio definition | dB |
| 12 | $\text{Sensitivity}_{\text{total}} = S_b \cdot A_v \cdot \alpha \cdot R_0$ | **End-to-end system sensitivity** (V / °C) | V / °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This architecture is the **backbone of every industrial process-control loop** — from HVAC duct sensors to silicon-fabrication cleanroom thermometry to petrochemical reactor skin-temperature monitoring. The same bridge-amplifier topology is used for **strain gauges, load cells, and pressure transducers**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — General Bridge Output Voltage

Consider a standard **DC-excited Wheatstone Bridge** with four arms:

- **Arm 1 (top-left):** Resistor R₁
- **Arm 2 (top-right):** Resistor R₂  
- **Arm 3 (bottom-left):** RTD with resistance $R_x = R(T)$
- **Arm 4 (bottom-right):** Resistor R₃

Excitation voltage $V_s$ is applied across the top node (between R₁ and R₂) and the bottom node (between $R_x$ and R₃). The differential output $V_{out}$ is taken between node **A** (between R₁ and $R_x$) and node **B** (between R₂ and R₃).

**Step 1: Voltage divider for the left arm** (R₁ in series with $R_x$).

$$V_A = V_s \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_1 + R_x}$$

**Step 2: Voltage divider for the right arm** (R₂ in series with R₃).

$$V_B = V_s \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

**Step 3: Differential output** between the two mid-points.

$$V_{out} = V_A - V_B = V_s \left[ \frac{R_x}{R_1 + R_x} - \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \right]$$

This is the **general un-balanced bridge equation** valid for any set of arm values.

**Step 4: Balance condition.** The bridge is "balanced" (V_out = 0) when the two bracketed terms are equal.

$$\frac{R_x}{R_1 + R_x} = \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R_x \cdot R_2 = R_3 \cdot R_1$$

This gives the standard balance relation:

$$R_x = \frac{R_1 \cdot R_3}{R_2}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linearized Small-Signal Approximation

Let us assume a **symmetric bridge** where $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$ and the RTD at temperature T₀ has resistance $R_x = R$. At a new temperature $T = T_0 + \Delta T$, the RTD changes to $R_x = R + \Delta R$.

**Step 1: Substitute into the general equation.**

$$V_{out} = V_s \left[ \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right]$$

**Step 2: Combine over a common denominator.**

$$V_{out} = V_s \left[ \frac{2(R + \Delta R) - (2R + \Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)} \right]$$

**Step 3: Simplify the numerator.**

$$V_{out} = V_s \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$

**Step 4: Factor out the denominator and use $\Delta R \ll R$** (small-signal condition).

$$V_{out} = V_s \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \frac{\Delta R}{2R}}$$

**Step 5: Apply the binomial approximation** $\dfrac{1}{1 + x} \approx 1 - x$ for small x.

$$V_{out} \approx V_s \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R} \left[ 1 - \frac{\Delta R}{2R} \right]$$

**Step 6: Drop the second-order term** since $\Delta R \ll R$.

$$\boxed{V_{out} \approx V_s \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}}$$

This is the **linearized bridge output** used by KTU in lab calculations. The **non-linearity error** introduced by dropping the second-order term is bounded by $\dfrac{\Delta R}{2R}$ — typically **≤ 0.5 %** for a Pt100 over 0–100 °C.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bridge Sensitivity

Sensitivity is defined as the rate of change of output voltage with respect to the transducer resistance.

**Step 1: Start from the general expression.**

$$V_{out}(R_x) = V_s \left[ \frac{R_x}{R_1 + R_x} - \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \right]$$

**Step 2: Differentiate with respect to $R_x$** (only the first term depends on $R_x$).

$$S_b = \frac{dV_{out}}{dR_x} = V_s \cdot \frac{d}{dR_x}\left[\frac{R_x}{R_1 + R_x}\right]$$

**Step 3: Apply the quotient rule** $\frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{u}{v}\right] = \frac{u'v - uv'}{v^2}$.

$$S_b = V_s \cdot \frac{(1)(R_1 + R_x) - (R_x)(1)}{(R_1 + R_x)^2} = V_s \cdot \frac{R_1}{(R_1 + R_x)^2}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate at the symmetric balance point** $R_1 = R_x = R$.

$$S_b^{\max} = \frac{V_s \cdot R}{(2R)^2} = \frac{V_s}{4R}$$

**Step 5: Combine with the RTD linear model** $\Delta R = \alpha R_0 \Delta T$ to get temperature sensitivity.

$$S_T = \frac{dV_{out}}{dT} = S_b \cdot \frac{dR}{dT} = \frac{V_s}{4R_0} \cdot \alpha R_0 = \frac{V_s \cdot \alpha}{4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example:** For Vs = 5 V and Pt100 (α = 0.00385 /°C), **$S_T = 5 \times 0.00385 / 4 = 4.81$ mV / °C** — a typical figure observed in the KTU lab.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier Gain

**Step 1: Input stage.** The two input op-amps (A1, A2) form a balanced differential pair with the gain-setting resistor $R_g$ between their inverting inputs.

Voltage across $R_g$ equals $(V_1 - V_2)$ (the differential input). Current through $R_g$ is:

$$I = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}$$

**Step 2: This same current flows through the two feedback resistors** $R_1$ (one in each input op-amp), producing a differential output:

$$V_3 - V_4 = (V_1 - V_2) + 2 I R_1 = (V_1 - V_2) \left[ 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g} \right]$$

**Step 3: Difference amplifier stage (A3).** The unity-gain subtractor produces:

$$V_{out,IA} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot (V_2 - V_1)$$

(assuming matched resistor pairs R₂=R₃ in the difference stage). The differential gain is therefore:

$$\boxed{A_v = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2}}$$

**Step 4: Design example.** To achieve $A_v = 100$ with $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = 10$ kΩ:

$$100 = \left(1 + \frac{20000}{R_g}\right) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R_g = \frac{20000}{99} \approx 202 \, \Omega$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — End-to-End Transfer Function

Combining the linearized bridge output and the instrumentation amplifier gain:

$$\boxed{V_{out,total} = \underbrace{\frac{V_s \cdot \alpha \cdot R_0}{4R_0}}_{\text{Bridge}} \cdot \underbrace{\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2}}_{\text{IA Gain}} \cdot \Delta T}$$

For the numerical example: Vs = 5 V, α = 0.00385, $A_v = 100$, ΔT = 100 °C:

$$V_{out,total} = 4.81 \text{ mV/°C} \times 100 \times 100 \text{ °C} = 4.81 \text{ V}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Bridge Amplifier Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph src["Excitation Source"]
        vs[DC Voltage Source Vs = 5V or 10V]
    end

    subgraph sensor["Temperature Transducer"]
        rtd[Pt100 RTD or NTC Thermistor]
    end

    subgraph bridge["Wheatstone Bridge Network"]
        r1[Arm R1 fixed]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of an Instrumentation Amplifier. Why is high CMRR essential in a bridge amplifier used for temperature measurement?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
CMRR is defined as the ratio of differential-mode gain $A_d$ to common-mode gain $A_{cm}$, expressed in decibels:

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$$

In a bridge amplifier, the differential millivolt signal (e.g., **4.81 mV/°C**) sits on top of a **large common-mode voltage of ≈ Vs/2** (≈ 2.5 V for a 5 V supply). A high CMRR (typically ≥ 80 dB) ensures that any noise, drift, or supply ripple common to both input nodes is rejected, and only the true differential temperature signal is amplified. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for bridge-context explanation)**

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the balance condition of a Wheatstone Bridge. What happens to the bridge output voltage when the RTD temperature increases by 10 °C in a balanced bridge?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Balance condition:** The bridge output is zero when the products of opposite arms are equal: $R_1 \cdot R_3 = R_2 \cdot R_x$, or equivalently $R_x = \dfrac{R_1 R_3}{R_2}$. **(1.5 marks)**

When temperature increases by 10 °C, the Pt100 RTD resistance increases by $\Delta R = \alpha R_0 \Delta T = 0.00385 \times 100 \times 10 = 3.85 \text{ Ω}$. The bridge becomes unbalanced, and the differential output becomes:

$$V_{out} \approx V_s \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R} = 5 \cdot \frac{3.85}{400} = 48.1 \text{ mV}$$

This **48.1 mV** signal is then amplified by the IA. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the output voltage of an unbalanced Wheatstone Bridge with a single varying arm. Show the linearized approximation valid for small resistance changes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **three-op-amp Instrumentation Amplifier** and derive its closed-loop gain expression. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Bridge Output Derivation (7 Marks)

**(i) General bridge equation (3 marks):** Applying the voltage divider rule to both arms of the bridge (R₁ in series with $R_x$ on the left; R₂ in series with R₃ on the right):

$$V_A = V_s \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_1 + R_x}, \qquad V_B = V_s \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

The differential output is:

$$V_{out} = V_A - V_B = V_s \left[ \frac{R_x}{R_1 + R_x} - \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \right]$$

**(ii) Balance condition (1 mark):** Setting $V_{out} = 0$ yields $R_x R_2 = R_1 R_3$, i.e., $R_x = \dfrac{R_1 R_3}{R_2}$.

**(iii) Symmetric and linearized form (3 marks):** With $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$ and $R_x = R + \Delta R$:

$$V_{out} = V_s \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)} = V_s \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \frac{\Delta R}{2R}}$$

For small $\Delta R$ (i.e., $\Delta R \ll R$), apply the binomial approximation:

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_s \cdot \Delta R}{4R}$$

The **non-linearity error** is bounded by $\dfrac{\Delta R}{2R}$.

#### Part (b) — Instrumentation Amplifier (7 Marks)

**(i) Block diagram and operation (3 marks):** A three-op-amp IA consists of two input buffer stages (A1, A2) with a shared gain-setting resistor $R_g$, followed by a unity-gain difference amplifier (A3). The input buffers provide **high input impedance**, while A3 provides **common-mode rejection**.

**(ii) Gain derivation (4 marks):** The differential input voltage $V_1 - V_2$ appears across $R_g$, producing a current $I = (V_1 - V_2)/R_g$ that flows through both feedback resistors $R_1$. The output of the first stage is:

$$V_3 - V_4 = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$

The difference amplifier then yields:

$$V_{out,IA} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot (V_2 - V_1)$$

Therefore the closed-loop differential gain is:

$$\boxed{A_v = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Design a bridge amplifier for measuring temperature in the range **0 to 100 °C** using a **Pt100 RTD**. Use excitation $V_s = 5$ V and IA gain $A_v = 100$. Compute the expected output voltage at 50 °C and 100 °C. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the sources of **non-linearity error** in a bridge amplifier and explain two methods for its compensation. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Design Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:** Pt100 RTD ($R_0 = 100 \text{ Ω}$, $\alpha = 0.00385 \text{ /°C}$), $V_s = 5$ V, $A_v = 100$.

**(i) RTD resistance at 50 °C (1.5 marks):**

$$R(50) = 100 \left(1 + 0.00385 \times 50\right) = 100 \times 1.1925 = 119.25 \text{ Ω}$$

**(ii) ΔR from balance (1 mark):** $\Delta R = 19.25 \text{ Ω}$

**(iii) Bridge output at 50 °C — linearized (1.5 marks):**

$$V_{out,bridge} = V_s \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R_0} = 5 \times \frac{19.25}{400} = 240.6 \text{ mV}$$

**(iv) Bridge output at 100 °C (1 mark):** $\Delta R = 38.5 \text{ Ω}$, so $V_{out,bridge} = 5 \times \dfrac{38.5}{400} = 481.25 \text{ mV}$.

**(v) Amplified outputs (2 marks):**

$$V_{out,IA}(50°C) = 100 \times 240.6 \text{ mV} = 24.06 \text{ V} \quad \text{(exceeds typical ADC range!)}$$

This indicates $A_v = 100$ is too high. A better choice is $A_v = 10$, yielding **2.4 V at 50 °C** and **4.8 V at 100 °C** — well-matched to a 0–5 V ADC.

#### Part (b) — Non-Linearity Compensation (7 Marks)

**(i) Sources of non-linearity (3 marks):**
1. **Bridge non-linearity:** The denominator $1 + \dfrac{\Delta R}{2R}$ in the bridge equation introduces curvature.
2. **RTD non-linearity:** Platinum deviates from perfectly linear above ~200 °C due to the BT² term.
3. **Amplifier non-linearity:** Op-amp gain errors and offset drift over temperature.
4. **Self-heating error:** Excitation current dissipates power $I^2 R$ in the RTD, raising its temperature above ambient.

**(ii) Compensation methods (4 marks):**

**Method 1 — Constant-Current Excitation:** Replace the voltage source with a **precision current source** $I_s$. Then $V_{out} = I_s \cdot \Delta R$, which is **exactly linear** in temperature since $I_s$ is constant and $R_x$ is the only variable. This eliminates the bridge non-linearity entirely.

**Method 2 — Software Look-Up Table (LUT):** In digital systems, store calibration data $V_{out}(T)$ at multiple known temperatures (e.g., every 10 °C) in microcontroller memory. Use polynomial curve-fitting or piecewise linear interpolation to correct residual non-linearity in software.

**Method 3 — Active Bridge Linearization:** Insert an op-amp in a **negative-feedback configuration** that mathematically cancels the $\Delta R / 2R$ term.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** use the general bridge equation when the question says "linearized" — the simplified formula $V_s \Delta R / 4R$ is expected, and full-equation answers lose **1–2 marks** for not addressing the small-signal approximation.
> 2. **Do NOT** forget to specify **units** in every numerical answer. Marks are deducted for bare numerical values.
> 3. **Do NOT** confuse the **bridge sensitivity** $S_b$ (V/Ω) with the **system sensitivity** $S_T$ (V/°C). The latter includes the RTD coefficient α.
> 4. **Do NOT** draw the IA as a single op-amp block. KTU specifically expects the **three-op-amp topology** with $R_g$ between the two input buffers.
> 5. **CMRR** must be reported in **dB**, not as a dimensionless ratio.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Bridge Equation (General):** $V_{out} = V_s \left[ \dfrac{R_x}{R_1+R_x} - \dfrac{R_3}{R_2+R_3} \right]$
- [x] **Balance Condition:** $R_1 R_3 = R_2 R_x$ → output is **zero**
- [x] **Linearized form:** $V_{out} \approx \dfrac{V_s \cdot \Delta R}{4R}$ valid when $\Delta R \ll R$
- [x] **Pt100 coefficients:** $R_0 = 100 \text{ Ω}$, $\alpha = 0.00385 \text{ /°C}$
- [x] **RTD Model:** $R(T) = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$ — linear in 0–100 °C range
- [x] **Bridge Sensitivity (symmetric):** $S_b^{\max} = \dfrac{V_s}{4R}$
- [x] **IA Gain (3-op-amp):** $A_v = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$
- [x] **CMRR Definition:** $20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$ — must be ≥ **80 dB** for precision work
- [x] **Linearization Methods:** constant-current excitation, software LUT, active bridge
- [x] **Typical Sensitivity:** **≈ 4.81 mV/°C** for Vs = 5 V, Pt100
- [x] **End-to-end Output:** Multiply bridge output by IA gain — final range should match ADC input (0–5 V or 0–10 V)
- [x] **Lab Procedure Order:** Assemble → Balance → Amplify → Calibrate → Plot
- [x] **Common Sensors Used:** Pt100, Pt1000, NTC Thermistor, LM35 (with bridge interface)
- [x] **IC Equivalents for IA:** **AD620**, **INA128**, **LM324** (discrete), **µA725**
