<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Thermistor

A **thermistor** (a portmanteau of *thermal* and *resistor*) is a passive, solid-state, highly temperature-sensitive semiconductor device whose electrical resistance varies predictably and non-linearly with its absolute body temperature. Thermistors are broadly classified into two families:

- **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient) Thermistor** — Resistance **decreases** as temperature rises. The dominant type used in instrumentation labs.
- **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient) Thermistor** — Resistance **increases** with temperature. Used in over-current protection, not in precision measurement.

For the KTU PCCPL308 experiment, the device under test (DUT) is invariably an **NTC thermistor**, typically with a nominal resistance of **10 kΩ at 25 °C** (denoted **10K-NTC**).

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Calibration

**Calibration** is the documented process of comparing the output of an instrument (here, the resistance $R$ of the thermistor) against a reference standard of known accuracy (here, a calibrated mercury/PT100 RTD reference or a precision dry-block calibrator), under controlled conditions, and establishing the mathematical relationship that allows the instrument's output to be converted into the true measurand (here, temperature $T$ in °C or K).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Breathing Sponge"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Breathing Sponge**
> Imagine a microscopic sponge soaked in charge carriers (electrons/holes). When the sponge is **cold**, the charge carriers are sluggish and "stuck" to the atoms — it conducts poorly, so resistance is **HIGH**. When the sponge is **heated**, the carriers gain thermal energy, break free, and move easily — it conducts well, so resistance **DROPS**. A thermistor behaves exactly like this "breathing sponge," and calibration is the process of measuring exactly *how fast* it breathes at every temperature so we can convert the breathing rate (resistance) back into the actual room temperature.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standards

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Reference Temperature | $T_0$ | **298.15 K (25 °C)** |
| Beta Constant (typical 10K NTC) | $\beta$ | **3950 K** |
| Nominal Resistance at 25 °C | $R_0$ | **10 000 Ω (10 kΩ)** |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k$ | **$1.380649 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** |
| Standard Reference (PT100) | RTD | **100 Ω at 0 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme requires students to clearly distinguish between **calibration** (determining corrections to a measuring instrument by comparison with a standard) and **adjustment** (physically tuning the instrument). Calibration of a thermistor **does NOT** alter the device; it only **builds a lookup table or equation** mapping $R \to T$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind NTC Thermistor Response

The NTC response arises from **semiconductor band-gap physics**. As temperature increases:

1. More valence electrons gain enough thermal energy ($E \geq E_g$) to jump into the conduction band.
2. The carrier concentration $n$ increases **exponentially** with temperature.
3. Conductivity $\sigma = n \cdot q \cdot (\mu_e + \mu_h)$ therefore increases, meaning resistance **drops exponentially** with temperature.

The empirical equation that captures this behavior with high accuracy (within $\pm 0.5\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ over a 0–100 °C span) is the **β-parameter (Beta) equation**.

## 2.2 The Two Governing Equations

### Equation 1 — The β-Parameter Equation (Two-Point Calibration)

$$
\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{\beta}\ln\left(\frac{R}{R_0}\right) + \frac{1}{T_0}
$$

- Valid when $T$ and $T_0$ are in **Kelvin**.
- $\beta$ is a material constant in **Kelvins**, typically 3000–5000 K.
- Rearranged to solve for resistance at any temperature:

$$
R(T) = R_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]
$$

### Equation 2 — The Steinhart–Hart Equation (Three-Point Calibration, Higher Accuracy)

$$
\frac{1}{T} = A + B\ln(R) + C\bigl[\ln(R)\bigr]^3
$$

- $A$, $B$, $C$ are Steinhart–Hart coefficients obtained by measuring $R$ at **three known temperatures** (e.g., 0 °C, 50 °C, 100 °C) and solving a $3 \times 3$ linear system.
- This is the **industry-standard** model for high-accuracy thermistor linearization.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Unit | Valid Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_T = R_0\,\exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\tfrac{1}{T}-\tfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$ | Resistance at temperature $T$ | Ω | −55 °C to +150 °C |
| 2 | $\beta = \dfrac{\ln(R_1/R_2)}{\bigl(\tfrac{1}{T_1}-\tfrac{1}{T_2}\bigr)}$ | β-constant from two calibration points | K | Constant for a given thermistor |
| 3 | $\alpha = -\dfrac{\beta}{T^2}$ | Temperature coefficient of resistance (TCR) | K⁻¹ | Always **negative** for NTC |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{T} = A + B\ln R + C(\ln R)^3$ | Steinhart–Hart inverse model | K | 0–100 °C typical |
| 5 | $T(K) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$ | Kelvin–Celsius conversion | K | Universal |
| 6 | $\Delta T = T_{\text{true}} - T_{\text{indicated}}$ | Calibration correction | °C | Tabulated at each point |
| 7 | $\text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{dR}{dT} = -\dfrac{\beta R}{T^2}$ | Local slope of R-T curve | Ω/K | Varies with T |
| 8 | $\text{Non-linearity}\,\% = \dfrac{\Delta R_{\max}}{R_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$ | Static error in calibration | % | Computed at mid-scale |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why all temperatures in Kelvin?** The β-equation is derived from the Boltzmann distribution $n \propto \exp(-E_g/2kT)$, which requires **absolute temperature**. Using °C in the β-equation is the **#1 mistake** students make in KTU viva — it produces grossly wrong answers and is heavily penalized.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical**: Disposable clinical thermometers (oral/axillary probes).
- **Automotive**: Engine coolant, intake air, and oil-temperature sensing.
- **Industrial**: HVAC duct sensors, 3D-printer hot-ends, battery-pack management in EVs.
- **Consumer**: Digital weather stations, smartphone battery protection, rice cookers, and incubators.
- **Aerospace & Spacecraft**: Cryogenic propellant level sensing using ultra-low-temperature NTCs.

The accuracy of every one of these systems depends entirely on the **calibration procedure** we are studying in this experiment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: The β-Equation from Semiconductor Carrier Statistics

**Starting point:** Intrinsic carrier concentration in a semiconductor follows the Boltzmann law,

$$
n_i \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2kT}\right)
$$

Since the NTC thermistor is fabricated from a heavily doped semiconductor where conductivity $\sigma \propto n_i$, the resistance (which is the inverse of conductance) becomes:

$$
R(T) \propto \frac{1}{\sigma} \propto \frac{1}{n_i} \propto \exp\!\left(\frac{E_g}{2kT}\right)
$$

Define the material constant $\beta \equiv \dfrac{E_g}{2k}$ (in Kelvin). Then:

$$
R(T) = C_1 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{\beta}{T}\right)
$$

Apply the boundary condition at the reference temperature $T_0$ where $R = R_0$:

$$
R_0 = C_1 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{\beta}{T_0}\right)
$$

Divide the two equations to eliminate $C_1$:

$$
\frac{R(T)}{R_0} = \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]
$$

**Final compact form (the β-equation):**

$$
\boxed{\,R(T) = R_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]\,}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation B: Determination of β from Two Calibration Points

Suppose the thermistor is calibrated at two known temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$, and the corresponding measured resistances are $R_1$ and $R_2$. We have:

$$
R_1 = R_0\,\exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]
$$

$$
R_2 = R_0\,\exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]
$$

Take the **ratio** to eliminate $R_0$:

$$
\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]
$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{R_1}{R_2}\right) = \beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)
$$

Solve for $\beta$:

$$
\boxed{\,\beta = \frac{\ln(R_1/R_2)}{\bigl(\tfrac{1}{T_1} - \tfrac{1}{T_2}\bigr)}\,}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Lab Data)

**Given:** A 10 kΩ NTC thermistor is placed in a temperature-controlled water bath. The following readings were taken against a calibrated PT100 reference:

| Point | Reference Temp $T$ (°C) | $T$ (K) | Measured $R$ (Ω) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 | 303.15 | 8 213 |
| 2 | 60 | 333.15 | 2 940 |
| 3 | 90 | 363.15 | 1 252 |

**Step 1 — Compute β using points 1 and 2:**

$$
\beta = \frac{\ln(8213/2940)}{\bigl(\tfrac{1}{303.15} - \tfrac{1}{333.15}\bigr)} = \frac{\ln(2.7936)}{0.0002970}
$$

Compute numerator:

$$
\ln(2.7936) = 1.0274
$$

Compute denominator:

$$
\frac{1}{303.15} - \frac{1}{333.15} = 0.0032988 - 0.0030017 = 0.0002971
$$

Therefore:

$$
\beta = \frac{1.0274}{0.0002971} \approx 3458\ \text{K}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify the model at point 3 (90 °C) using $R_0$ and $T_0$ from the data sheet (10 kΩ at 298.15 K):**

$$
R_{\text{pred}}(363.15) = 10000 \cdot \exp\!\left[3458\!\left(\frac{1}{363.15} - \frac{1}{298.15}\right)\right]
$$

Compute bracket:

$$
\frac{1}{363.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} = 0.0027536 - 0.0033540 = -0.0006004
$$

Multiply by β:

$$
3458 \times (-0.0006004) = -2.0762
$$

Exponentiate:

$$
R_{\text{pred}} = 10000 \times e^{-2.0762} = 10000 \times 0.1255 = 1255\ \Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Error analysis:**

$$
\text{Error} = \vert R_{\text{meas}} - R_{\text{pred}} \vert = \vert 1252 - 1255 \vert = 3\ \Omega
$$

$$
\text{Percentage Error} = \frac{3}{1252} \times 100 \approx 0.24\%
$$

**Step 4 — Convert error in resistance to equivalent temperature error** (using sensitivity at 90 °C):

$$
\left.\frac{dR}{dT}\right|_{T=363.15} = -\frac{\beta R}{T^2} = -\frac{3458 \times 1252}{363.15^2} = -32.83\ \Omega/\text{K}
$$

$$
\Delta T \approx \frac{\Delta R}{dR/dT} = \frac{3}{32.83} \approx 0.09\ ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Conclusion:** The β-model predicts temperature with an error of only $\pm 0.09\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ at 90 °C, which is well within the $\pm 0.5\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ accuracy specified by KTU.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — β vs. Steinhart–Hart

| Property | β-Equation | Steinhart–Hart |
|---|---|---|
| Number of calibration points required | **2** (minimum) | **3** (minimum) |
| Accuracy over 0–100 °C | $\pm 0.5$ to $\pm 1.0\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $\pm 0.01$ to $\pm 0.1\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| Computational complexity | Single $\exp$ | One $\ln$ + polynomial |
| Suitability for KTU lab | **Yes (preferred)** | Optional / advanced |
| Extrapolation outside calibrated range | **Poor** | Better |
| Practical use in industry | Simple OEM modules | Scientific & medical |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Calibration Setup

The following Mermaid block diagram maps every functional element of the experimental setup, including signal flow, energy flow, and the human/data interaction points. This is the diagram you should reproduce in your KTU lab record.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A[Reference Standard]
        refStd[PT100 RTD Calibrated Reference]
        refDisp[Reference Temperature Display]
    end

    subgraph B[Device Under Test - DUT]
        ncBath[Constant Temperature Water / Oil Bath]
        thermistor[10K NTC Thermistor]
        wires[Shielded 4-Wire Leads]
    end

    subgraph C[Measurement Chain]
        dmm[Precision Digital Multimeter - Ohms Mode]
        adc[Data Acquisition Card - Optional]
    end

    subgraph D[Data Processing and Calibration]
        tab[Calibration Table: T vs R]
        beta[Fit Beta Constant by Linear Regression]
        sh[Optional: Fit Steinhart-Hart A B C]
        curve[R versus T Calibration Curve]
    end

    refStd --> refDisp
    ncBath --> thermistor
    thermistor --> wires
    wires --> dmm
    dmm --> tab
    refDisp --> tab
    tab --> beta
    tab --> sh
    beta --> curve
    sh --> curve
```

## 4.2 Operational Flow Chart of the Calibration Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start of Experiment]) --> prep[Prepare Water Bath and Reference PT100]
    prep --> ice[Set Bath to 0 deg C Using Ice Slurry]
    ice --> eq1[Allow 5 Minutes for Thermal Equilibrium]
    eq1 --> r1[Record R1 from DMM and T1 from PT100]
    r1 --> next1[Raise Bath to Next Calibration Point]
    next1 --> eq2[Wait for Equilibrium]
    eq2 --> r2[Record R2 and T2]
    r2 --> more{All Points Completed?}
    more -->|No| next1
    more -->|Yes| fit[Compute Beta from All Pairs and Average]
    fit --> plot[Plot Measured R vs T and Fitted Curve]
    plot --> err[Compute Maximum Non-Linearity Error]
    err --> report[Prepare Calibration Certificate]
    report --> stop([End of Experiment])
```

## 4.3 Detailed Functional Pin-Out of the Measurement Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NTC[10K NTC Probe]
        Rntc[Variable Resistor R_T]
    end

    subgraph BR[Precision Bridge / Voltage Divider]
        R1[Fixed Resistor R_ref = 10K 0.1 percent]
        R2[Fixed Resistor R_ref = 10K 0.1 percent]
        R3[Fixed Resistor R_ref = 10K 0.1 percent]
    end

    subgraph V[Excitation Source]
        Vsrc[Constant 5.000 V DC Source]
    end

    subgraph M[Readout]
        DMM[DMM in Voltage Mode]
    end

    Vsrc --> R1
    R1 --> Rntc
    Rntc --> GND1[Ground Node]
    Vsrc --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> GND1
    Rntc --> DMM
    R3 --> DMM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Tip:** A Wheatstone bridge with **three fixed precision resistors** is preferred over a simple voltage divider because it inherently cancels the self-heating error and the lead-resistance error. Self-heating of the thermistor must be kept below **1 mW** by limiting the excitation current to well under **1 mA** through a 10 kΩ sensor.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
**Define a thermistor. Differentiate between NTC and PTC types in two points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A thermistor is a temperature-sensitive semiconductor resistor whose resistance changes predictably with temperature.
- **NTC (1 mark):** Negative Temperature Coefficient — resistance **decreases** with increase in temperature. Used for temperature measurement and inrush current limiting.
- **PTC (1 mark):** Positive Temperature Coefficient — resistance **increases** with temperature. Used as resettable fuses and motor overheat protection.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
**Why is an ice-water slurry used as one of the calibration points in the thermistor experiment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1) The ice-water slurry provides the **ice point**, i.e., exactly **0 °C (273.15 K)**, the fixed point of the International Temperature Scale of 1990 (ITS-90).
- (1) It is **reproducible** in any lab without needing a costly standard, and the bath's large thermal mass holds 0 °C stably for several minutes.
- (1) It provides a low-temperature anchor that, together with a second high-temperature point (e.g., boiling water, 100 °C), allows the β-constant to be computed accurately and the calibration curve to be extrapolated safely across the working range.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting Kelvin** in the β-equation ⇒ 0 marks for the calculation. Always convert °C → K first.
> 2. **Plugging the wrong sign** in $\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)$ ⇒ negative resistance error. Remember: for NTC, $T > T_0 \Rightarrow R < R_0$.
> 3. **Not tabulating raw data** before calculation ⇒ −2 marks for "missing methodology."
> 4. **Ignoring self-heating**: Always state that excitation current was kept **< 1 mA** and excitation power **< 1 mW**.
> 5. **No units in the final answer** ⇒ −1 mark per the strict KTU 2024 marking scheme.
> 6. **Linearizing a non-linear curve without justification** ⇒ Mention that thermistor response is inherently exponential; linearization (e.g., by parallel resistor $R_p$) is a design choice, not a calibration step.

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled block diagram, describe the experimental setup for calibrating an NTC thermistor against a PT100 reference standard. List the apparatus required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10 kΩ NTC thermistor has $\beta = 3950$ K and $R_0 = 10\,000\ \Omega$ at 25 °C. Calculate (i) its resistance at 80 °C, and (ii) its temperature coefficient of resistance at 50 °C. Comment on the sensitivity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Setup (7 marks):**

**Apparatus (3 marks):** 10 kΩ NTC thermistor (DUT), calibrated PT100 RTD with indicator, constant-temperature water bath (or oil bath up to 100 °C), precision digital multimeter (DMM) in ohms mode, ice-water slurry, boiling-water flask, connecting leads, insulating tongs.

**Procedure (3 marks):**

1. Immerse the thermistor and the PT100 reference in the same water bath ensuring their tips are at the same depth and not touching the bath wall.
2. Set the bath to the first calibration point (0 °C using ice slurry), wait 5 min for thermal equilibrium.
3. Read $T_1$ from the PT100 indicator and $R_1$ from the DMM simultaneously. Record in tabular form.
4. Repeat for at least 5 more points, e.g., 25 °C, 50 °C, 75 °C, 100 °C (use boiling water for 100 °C).
5. Plot $R$ vs $T$, fit the β-equation, and tabulate deviations.

**Block diagram reference (1 mark):** See Section 4.1 above (mark awarded for any correct block diagram showing Reference, DUT, Measurement, and Data Processing blocks).

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $R_0 = 10\,000\ \Omega$, $\beta = 3950$ K, $T_0 = 298.15$ K.

**(i) Resistance at $T = 80\ ^\circ\text{C} = 353.15$ K (3 marks):**

$$
R(353.15) = 10000 \times \exp\!\left[3950\left(\frac{1}{353.15} - \frac{1}{298.15}\right)\right]
$$

$$
\frac{1}{353.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} = 0.002832 - 0.003354 = -0.000522
$$

$$
3950 \times (-0.000522) = -2.062
$$

$$
R(80\,^{\circ}\text{C}) = 10000 \times e^{-2.062} = 10000 \times 0.1272 = 1272\ \Omega
$$

**(ii) TCR at 50 °C = 323.15 K (4 marks):**

$$
\alpha = -\frac{\beta}{T^2} = -\frac{3950}{(323.15)^2} = -\frac{3950}{104425.9} = -0.03783\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

Sensitivity magnitude:

$$
\left.\frac{dR}{dT}\right|_{T=323.15} = -\alpha R(50\,^{\circ}\text{C}) = 0.03783 \times 3595 \approx -136\ \Omega/^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Comment (1 mark, included in the 4):** A TCR of $\approx -3.78\%/\text{K}$ at 50 °C is **~10× greater** than a platinum RTD (≈ 0.385 %/K), confirming that thermistors are extremely sensitive at low-to-mid temperatures, which is why they are preferred for biomedical and HVAC applications. However, the high non-linearity mandates proper calibration.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the β-parameter equation for an NTC thermistor starting from the Boltzmann carrier-concentration relation. Define each symbol and state its unit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During calibration, an NTC thermistor recorded $R = 5\,860\ \Omega$ at $T_1 = 40\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and $R = 2\,490\ \Omega$ at $T_2 = 70\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Determine (i) the β-constant, (ii) the nominal resistance $R_0$ at 25 °C, and (iii) the expected resistance at 100 °C. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

See **Section 3.1** above for the complete derivation. The full marks breakdown is:

- Start with $n_i \propto \exp(-E_g/2kT)$ — **(1 mark)**
- Write $R(T) = C_1 \exp(\beta/T)$ with $\beta = E_g/2k$ — **(1 mark)**
- Apply boundary condition at $T_0$ to get $C_1$ — **(1 mark)**
- Divide the two equations to eliminate $C_1$ — **(1 mark)**
- Final form $R(T) = R_0 \exp[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)]$ — **(1 mark)**
- Define all four symbols ($R$, $R_0$, $T$, $T_0$, $\beta$) with correct units — **(2 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $T_1 = 40\ ^\circ\text{C} = 313.15$ K, $R_1 = 5\,860\ \Omega$; $T_2 = 70\ ^\circ\text{C} = 343.15$ K, $R_2 = 2\,490\ \Omega$; reference $T_0 = 25\ ^\circ\text{C} = 298.15$ K.

**(i) β-constant (3 marks):**

$$
\beta = \frac{\ln(5860/2490)}{\left(\frac{1}{313.15} - \frac{1}{343.15}\right)} = \frac{\ln(2.3534)}{0.0002807}
$$

$$
\ln(2.3534) = 0.8558
$$

$$
\beta = \frac{0.8558}{0.0002807} = 3049\ \text{K}
$$

**(ii) $R_0$ at 25 °C (2 marks):**

Use the β-equation solved backwards for $R_0$ from point 1:

$$
R_0 = \frac{R_1}{\exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{313.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} = 0.003193 - 0.003354 = -0.000161
$$

$$
3049 \times (-0.000161) = -0.4909
$$

$$
R_0 = \frac{5860}{e^{-0.4909}} = \frac{5860}{0.6121} = 9574\ \Omega \approx 9.57\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

(Verify with point 2 should give the same $R_0$ within experimental error — examiner's check.)

**(iii) Resistance at 100 °C = 373.15 K (2 marks):**

$$
R(373.15) = 9574 \times \exp\!\left[3049\left(\frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{298.15}\right)\right]
$$

$$
\frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} = 0.002680 - 0.003354 = -0.000674
$$

$$
3049 \times (-0.000674) = -2.055
$$

$$
R(100\,^{\circ}\text{C}) = 9574 \times e^{-2.055} = 9574 \times 0.1281 = 1226\ \Omega
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition**: Thermistor = *thermal + resistor*; an NTC's resistance **drops** as temperature rises.
- **Two equations you MUST know**: (1) β-equation, (2) Steinhart–Hart equation.
- **Always use Kelvin** in any thermistor equation. $T(K) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$.
- **β is always positive** for an NTC (typically 3000–5000 K). It is a *material* constant, not a function of temperature.
- **TCR ($\alpha$) is always negative** for NTC; magnitude $\alpha = \beta/T^2$, so sensitivity is *highest at low temperatures*.
- **Self-heating error** is the largest practical pitfall: keep excitation current **< 1 mA**, dissipation **< 1 mW**.
- **Calibration ≠ Adjustment**: Calibration builds a *table/equation*; adjustment physically changes the instrument.
- **Reference standards**: PT100 RTD (industrial) or mercury-in-glass thermometer (lab). KTU lab manuals prefer PT100.
- **Calibration points required**: minimum 2 for β-method, minimum 3 for Steinhart–Hart. Use **0 °C (ice slurry)** and **100 °C (boiling water)** as ITS-90 fixed points.
- **Three sources of error** to mention in viva: (1) self-heating, (2) lead resistance (use 4-wire Kelvin connection), (3) thermal lag (always wait for equilibrium ≥ 5 min).
- **Sensitivity check**: A 10 kΩ NTC has $dR/dT \approx -400\ \Omega/\text{K}$ at 25 °C, decreasing to about $-100\ \Omega/\text{K}$ at 100 °C.
- **Linearity**: Thermistor response is **inherently non-linear** (logarithmic). A single calibration polynomial is *not* advisable; either (i) accept non-linearity with β-equation, or (ii) add a **shunt resistor $R_p$** to linearize within a narrow band.
- **Plot you must draw**: $R$ (y-axis, Ω) vs $T$ (x-axis, °C) — exponential decay curve. Also plot $1/T$ (K⁻¹) vs $\ln R$ — should yield a **straight line** of slope $\beta$, used for least-squares fit.
- **Final viva line**: *"Calibration of an NTC thermistor establishes the inverse mapping $R \to T$ via either the two-point β-equation or the three-point Steinhart–Hart model, with temperature expressed in Kelvin and self-heating kept below 1 mW to preserve the stated accuracy of ±0.5 °C."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of Thermocouple for Temperature Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL308 - INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of Thermocouple for Temperature Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **thermocouple** is a passive temperature-sensing transducer consisting of two dissimilar electrical conductors joined at one end (the *measuring* or *hot junction*) and connected to a measuring instrument at the other end (the *reference* or *cold junction*). The temperature difference between the two junctions generates a small DC voltage (the **Seebeck EMF**) that is a reproducible, monotonic function of that temperature difference.

**Calibration of a thermocouple** is the experimental process of determining the functional relationship—typically a lookup table, polynomial fit, or graphical curve—between the measured thermoelectric EMF (in millivolts) and the actual temperature at the measuring junction, using a primary or secondary temperature standard as the reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Calibration is performed to compensate for individual thermocouple non-idealities, ageing effects (drift), and to verify conformity with the **IEC 60584-1** standard reference tables (or the equivalent **NIST ITS-90** thermoelectric voltage tables).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have **two metal rulers of different colours** sitting on a sunny windowsill. Both expand when heated, but at *slightly different rates* because they are made of different materials. If you join them firmly at one end (the *junction*) and pin the other ends to a fixed wall, the free end will drift *sideways* as the temperature changes, because one metal stretched more than the other.

A thermocouple works on the **same principle**, except instead of mechanical sideways movement, the two different metals produce a tiny **voltage (EMF)** that is measured by a sensitive millivolt meter. The bigger the temperature difference between the junction and the meter terminals, the bigger the voltage. **Calibration** is essentially writing the *ruler* of how many millivolts correspond to how many degrees Celsius.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - Standard reference temperature for tables: **T_ref = 0 °C (273.15 K)**, maintained using a **Triple-Point-of-Water cell** or an **ice bath** in lab practice.
> - Typical laboratory sensitivity of a **K-type (Chromel–Alumel)** thermocouple: **≈ 41 µV/°C** at room temperature.
> - Typical sensitivity of a **T-type (Copper–Constantan)** thermocouple: **≈ 40 µV/°C** at room temperature.
> - Standard reference document: **IEC 60584-1** (for thermoelectric voltages vs temperature).

## 1.3 Why Calibration is Necessary

A new thermocouple does not perfectly match the published reference tables because of:
1. **Material impurities** in the thermoelements.
2. **Manufacturing tolerances** (slight composition drift from nominal alloy).
3. **Ageing / drift** caused by oxidation, grain growth, and contamination at high temperatures.
4. **Cold-junction compensation errors** in the readout instrument.
5. **Lead-wire and connector contact resistance.**

Calibration quantifies these errors and produces a *correction curve* specific to that particular thermocouple.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physics — The Seebeck Effect

When two dissimilar metals **A** and **B** form a closed loop with their two junctions held at different absolute temperatures **T_hot** and **T_cold**, an electromotive force **E_AB** is generated around the loop. This is the **Seebeck Effect** (Thomas Johann Seebeck, 1821).

The magnitude of this EMF depends on:
- The *thermoelectric materials* (captured by the **Seebeck coefficient** **S_AB(T)**, in µV/°C).
- The *temperature difference* between the two junctions.

The thermoelectric voltage is mathematically given by the **Seebeck integral**:

$$
E_{AB}(T_{hot}, T_{cold}) \;=\; \int_{T_{cold}}^{T_{hot}} S_{AB}(T)\, dT
$$

## 2.2 The Three Fundamental Laws of Thermoelectric Circuits (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)

These three laws form the **theoretical foundation** of every thermocouple calibration. They are guaranteed questions in either Part A or Part B.

### Law 1 — Law of Homogeneous Materials
> A thermoelectric EMF cannot be generated in a circuit composed of *thermally homogeneous* conductors, no matter how the temperature is distributed along them.

**Implication:** A fault (hot spot) in *one* leg of the thermocouple wire does **not** produce a parasitic EMF as long as that leg is chemically homogeneous. This justifies using **extension/compensating wires** of the *same* thermoelectric identity as the thermocouple.

### Law 2 — Law of Intermediate Metals
> Inserting a third homogeneous conductor **C** into the circuit, with its two ends held at the *same* temperature, does **not** change the net EMF.

**Implication:** This is *the* law that allows a copper voltmeter, copper lead wires, and solder joints to be inserted into a thermocouple circuit without affecting the measurement. Crucial for calibration experiments.

### Law 3 — Law of Intermediate (Successive) Temperatures
$$
E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) \;=\; E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) \;+\; E_{AB}(T_2, T_3)
$$

**Implication:** If a thermocouple is calibrated for a reference junction at 0 °C, but the actual cold junction is at **T_2**, the true EMF for the desired hot temperature **T_1** can be computed by adding the EMF of the actual cold-junction temperature **T_2** (with respect to 0 °C) to the measured EMF. This is the **basis of all software/hardware cold-junction compensation** methods.

## 2.3 Principle of the Calibration Experiment

In the KTU PCCPL308 lab, calibration is performed by the **Comparison Method**:

1. The thermocouple's *measuring junction* is immersed in a **calibrated temperature bath** (e.g., a stirred water bath, oil bath, or dry-block calibrator) together with a **standard reference sensor** (typically a calibrated **mercury-in-glass thermometer** or a **PT100 RTD** with traceable certificate).
2. The *reference junction* of the thermocouple is maintained at **0 °C** in an **ice bath** (or its electrical equivalent in a modern digital readout).
3. The bath temperature is raised in **discrete steps** (e.g., every 10 °C from 30 °C to 100 °C).
4. At each step, after thermal equilibrium, the **reference temperature T_actual** and the **thermocouple EMF E** are recorded simultaneously.
5. A **calibration curve** of **E (mV) vs T_actual (°C)** is plotted.
6. The **deviation** between the measured EMF and the IEC 60584-1 standard value at that temperature is computed as the **correction**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $E_{AB} = \int_{T_{cold}}^{T_{hot}} S_{AB}(T)\, dT$ | Seebeck integral — fundamental EMF relation | mV |
| 2 | $E_{AB}(T_1,T_3) = E_{AB}(T_1,T_2) + E_{AB}(T_2,T_3)$ | Law of intermediate temperatures | mV |
| 3 | $S_{AB} = \dfrac{dE_{AB}}{dT}$ | Instantaneous Seebeck coefficient (sensitivity) | µV/°C |
| 4 | $T_{actual} = T_{indicated} + \Delta T_{correction}$ | Corrected temperature after calibration | °C |
| 5 | $\Delta T_{correction}(T) = T_{IEC\,table}(E) - T_{actual}$ | Per-point calibration correction | °C |
| 6 | $\% \text{ Error} = \dfrac{T_{indicated} - T_{actual}}{T_{actual}} \times 100$ | Percentage reading error | % |
| 7 | $E_{poly}(T) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i (T)^{i}$ | Polynomial fit of calibration curve | mV |
| 8 | $E_{CJC} = E(T_{hot}, 0^\circ C) - E(T_{cold}, 0^\circ C)$ | Cold-junction compensation formula | mV |
| 9 | $R_{PT100} = 100 \left[1 + 0.00385\,(T - 0)\right]$ | Reference RTD resistance (PT100, approximate linear) | Ω |
| 10 | $\text{Repeatability} = \pm \dfrac{(T_{max} - T_{min})}{2}$ at fixed bath | Static repeatability error | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Markdown Note:** All absolute-value bars have been written using $\vert$ or $\mid$ to keep the table parser happy. Do **not** use plain vertical pipes inside the table cells.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial furnace control** in steel, cement, and glass plants (K-type for ≤ 1100 °C, S/R-type for ≤ 1600 °C).
- **Gas-turbine inlet/exhaust temperature monitoring** in aerospace propulsion.
- **HVAC and building-management systems** (T-type for chilled-water and refrigeration lines).
- **Nuclear reactor coolant temperature profiling** with sheathed MgO-insulated thermocouples.
- **Food, pharma, and laboratory process validation** where periodic in-situ calibration against a dry-block calibrator is mandatory (ISO 17025 requirement).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Seebeck Integral to the Approximate Linear Model

Most introductory derivations of thermocouple EMF start with the integral form and simplify it for a small temperature range where the Seebeck coefficient is nearly constant.

**Step 1 — Start with the Seebeck integral.**

$$
E_{AB} \;=\; \int_{T_{cold}}^{T_{hot}} S_{AB}(T)\, dT
$$

**Step 2 — Assume constant sensitivity.**

For a *narrow* range, we treat $S_{AB}(T)$ as a constant $S_{AB}$ evaluated at the mean temperature.

$$
E_{AB} \;\approx\; S_{AB} \int_{T_{cold}}^{T_{hot}} dT
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$
E_{AB} \;\approx\; S_{AB} \left[\, T \,\right]_{T_{cold}}^{T_{hot}}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify to the linear model.**

$$
E_{AB} \;\approx\; S_{AB}\,\bigl(T_{hot} - T_{cold}\bigr) \;=\; S_{AB}\,\Delta T
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**

The thermocouple behaves like a *voltage source of sensitivity $S_{AB}$* whose output is proportional to the temperature difference. For a **K-type** thermocouple, $S_{AB} \approx 41 \,\mu V/°C$ at 25 °C, so a 100 °C temperature difference gives approximately **4.1 mV**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC) Using the Law of Intermediate Temperatures

Suppose the *desired* reference junction temperature for the table lookup is 0 °C, but the physical reference junction is at **T_cj = 30 °C** because the terminal block of the digital indicator is warm.

**Step 1 — Write the law of intermediate temperatures.**

$$
E_{AB}\bigl(T_{hot},\,0^\circ C\bigr) \;=\; E_{AB}\bigl(T_{hot},\,T_{cj}\bigr) \;+\; E_{AB}\bigl(T_{cj},\,0^\circ C\bigr)
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the three EMFs.**

- $E_{AB}(T_{hot},\,T_{cj})$ — the EMF *measured* by the voltmeter (what the instrument sees).
- $E_{AB}(T_{cj},\,0^\circ C)$ — the EMF of a *virtual* thermocouple with hot junction at $T_{cj}$ and cold at 0 °C (looked up from the standard table for the *current* terminal-block temperature).
- $E_{AB}(T_{hot},\,0^\circ C)$ — the EMF that the *table* expects for the true hot temperature.

**Step 3 — Rearrange to obtain the true hot-junction temperature.**

$$
E_{AB}\bigl(T_{hot},\,0^\circ C\bigr) \;=\; E_{measured} \;+\; E_{AB}\bigl(T_{cj},\,0^\circ C\bigr)
$$

**Step 4 — Use the table to recover temperature.**

Look up $E_{measured} + E_{AB}(T_{cj}, 0^\circ C)$ in the IEC 60584-1 reference table to find the *correct* $T_{hot}$.

**Engineering note:** This is exactly what every modern digital thermocouple indicator does internally — an additional sensor (often a silicon diode or another PT100) measures the terminal-block temperature, and a microcontroller adds the compensating EMF in firmware.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Computing the Calibration Correction

For a *given* data point $(T_{actual}, E_{measured})$:

**Step 1 — Read the standard table value.**

From the IEC 60584-1 table, find $E_{standard}$ corresponding to $T_{actual}$ with reference at 0 °C.

**Step 2 — Compute the deviation in EMF.**

$$
\Delta E \;=\; E_{measured} - E_{standard}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert EMF deviation to temperature deviation using local sensitivity.**

$$
\Delta T_{correction} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta E}{S_{AB}(T_{actual})}
$$

**Step 4 — Correct any future reading.**

$$
T_{corrected} \;=\; T_{indicated\,from\,table} \;-\; \Delta T_{correction}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key idea for the lab record:** A positive $\Delta E$ means the thermocouple reads *higher* EMF than a standard thermocouple would for the same temperature — therefore, when the indicator looks up the table with that EMF, it will report a temperature that is *too high*. The correction must be **subtracted**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical K-Type Calibration Data (Illustrative)

| Bath temperature $T_{actual}$ (°C) | Measured EMF $E_m$ (mV) | Standard EMF $E_s$ (mV) | $\Delta E = E_m - E_s$ (mV) | $\Delta T = \Delta E / S_{AB}$ (°C) | Corrected temperature (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 30 | 1.203 | 1.203 | 0.000 | 0.00 | 30.00 |
| 40 | 1.612 | 1.612 | 0.000 | 0.00 | 40.00 |
| 50 | 2.023 | 2.023 | 0.000 | 0.00 | 50.00 |
| 60 | 2.436 | 2.436 | 0.000 | 0.00 | 60.00 |
| 70 | 2.851 | 2.851 | 0.000 | 0.00 | 70.00 |
| 80 | 3.267 | 3.267 | 0.000 | 0.00 | 80.00 |
| 90 | 3.682 | 3.682 | 0.000 | 0.00 | 90.00 |
| 100 | 4.096 | 4.096 | 0.000 | 0.00 | 100.00 |

In practice, the *student's* column will differ from the standard by a few microvolts to a few tens of microvolts. The **shape** of $\Delta T$ vs $T_{actual}$ is the **calibration certificate** of that thermocouple.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the deviation is small in the table above:** The K-type thermocouple is highly linear in the 0–100 °C range, and a brand-new, high-quality K-type wire will track the IEC table within ±1 °C (often ±0.5 °C). Industrial "special limits" K-type wire is guaranteed to ±1.1 °C or ±0.4 % of reading, whichever is greater.

---

## 3.5 Sensitivity / Slope Calculation from Calibration Data

After recording the data, the student must report the **average sensitivity** of the calibrated thermocouple:

$$
\overline{S}_{AB} \;=\; \dfrac{E(T_{max}) - E(T_{min})}{T_{max} - T_{min}}
$$

**Worked example** (K-type, 30 °C to 100 °C, using the table above):

**Step 1 — Identify the end points.**

$$
E(30^\circ C) = 1.203 \text{ mV}, \quad E(100^\circ C) = 4.096 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the slope formula.**

$$
\overline{S}_{AB} \;=\; \dfrac{4.096 - 1.203}{100 - 30} \;=\; \dfrac{2.893}{70}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
\overline{S}_{AB} \;\approx\; 0.04133 \text{ mV/°C} \;=\; 41.33 \,\mu V/°C
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the standard.**

Standard K-type sensitivity over 0–100 °C ≈ **41.4 µV/°C**. Percentage deviation:

$$
\% \text{ Deviation} = \dfrac{41.33 - 41.40}{41.40} \times 100 \;\approx\; -0.17 \,\%
$$

This is well within the **±2.2 °C or ±0.75 %** accuracy class (standard limits) for a K-type thermocouple per IEC 60584-2.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs are purely alphanumeric and letter-prefixed (e.g., `tcA`, `bathB`, `iceC`).
- No reserved keywords used as node names.
- Node labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text only, with **no** bold/italic/HTML inside double-quoted labels.
- Used a **block-level functional architecture** mapping since a physical hand-drawn setup is impractical to render in Mermaid.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of the Calibration Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tcA["THERMOCOUPLE MEASURING JUNCTION"]
    bathB["CALIBRATED TEMPERATURE BATH WATER OR OIL STIRRED HEATER"]
    refC["REFERENCE SENSOR PT100 RTD OR MERCURY THERMOMETER"]
    iceD["ICE BATH REFERENCE JUNCTION ZERO DEGREE CELSIUS"]
    wireE["COMPENSATING LEADS COPPER TO DMM"]
    dmmF["DIGITAL MILLIVOLT METER DMM HIGH INPUT IMPEDANCE"]
    pcG["DATA LOGGER OR PC WITH SPREADSHEET"]
    tabH["IEC 60584 STANDARD REFERENCE TABLE"]

    bathB --- tcA
    bathB --- refC
    tcA --> iceD
    iceD --> wireE
    wireE --> dmmF
    refC --> dmmF
    dmmF --> pcG
    tabH -. lookup .- pcG
```

### Block-Level Reading of the Diagram

| Block | Role |
|-------|------|
| `tcA` | Sensing element — generates Seebeck EMF proportional to hot-junction temperature. |
| `bathB` | Provides a *uniform*, *stable* temperature environment common to both the thermocouple and the reference. |
| `refC` | Traceable standard thermometer; supplies the *true* temperature for the data point. |
| `iceD` | Holds the reference (cold) junction at exactly **0 °C** so that tabulated EMFs apply directly. |
| `wireE` | Copper extension leads carry the millivolt signal to the meter. |
| `dmmF` | High-impedance (≥ 10 MΩ) digital millivolt meter — does not load the thermocouple. |
| `pcG` | Records pairs $(T_{actual},\,E)$ for plotting the calibration curve. |
| `tabH` | Published reference EMF values used to compute the per-point correction. |

## 4.3 Mermaid — Calibration Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["STEP 1 PREPARE ICE BATH IN DEWAR FLASK"]
    s2["STEP 2 IMMERSE COLD JUNCTION IN ICE BATH"]
    s3["STEP 3 SET BATH TO INITIAL TEMPERATURE 30 DEG C"]
    s4["STEP 4 WAIT FOR THERMAL EQUILIBRIUM 5 TO 10 MIN"]
    s5["STEP 5 RECORD REFERENCE TEMPERATURE AND EMF"]
    s6["STEP 6 INCREMENT BATH BY 10 DEG C"]
    s7["STEP 7 REPEAT STEPS 4 TO 6 UP TO 100 DEG C"]
    s8["STEP 8 COMPUTE DELTA E AND DELTA T FOR EACH POINT"]
    s9["STEP 9 PLOT EMF VS TEMPERATURE CALIBRATION CURVE"]
    s10["STEP 10 PLOT DELTA T VS TEMPERATURE CORRECTION CURVE"]

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8 --> s9 --> s10
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Equipment list for the KTU PCCPL308 lab experiment:**
> 1. K-type or J-type thermocouple (test specimen).
> 2. Calibrated mercury thermometer (0–110 °C, 0.1 °C least count) **or** PT100 RTD with digital readout.
> 3. Thermostatically controlled water bath with stirrer (or hot-plate + beaker for lower accuracy).
> 4. Vacuum flask (thermos) packed with crushed ice + distilled water for the 0 °C reference.
> 5. High-impedance digital millivolt meter (resolution 1 µV).
> 6. Copper connecting leads and K-type compensating cable.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the Seebeck Effect. Why is it the working principle of a thermocouple?  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Seebeck Effect is the generation of an electromotive force (EMF) in a closed circuit made of two dissimilar conductors **A** and **B** when their two junctions are maintained at different temperatures.
- **1 mark** — Statement of the effect.
- **1 mark** — Identification that the EMF depends on the *material pair* and the *temperature difference* $\Delta T$.
- **1 mark** — Conclusion that this EMF is *reproducible* and *monotonic*, making it usable as a temperature transducer (a thermocouple).

### Q2. State any two laws of thermoelectric circuits.  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Law of Homogeneous Materials (1 mark):** A thermoelectric EMF cannot be produced in a homogeneous conductor regardless of the temperature distribution along it.
- **Law of Intermediate Metals (1 mark):** Inserting a third conductor into the circuit does not alter the net EMF as long as its two ends are at the same temperature.
- **Law of Intermediate Temperatures (1 mark, optional third):** The EMF corresponding to a temperature span $T_1$ to $T_3$ equals the sum of the EMFs for the spans $T_1$ to $T_2$ and $T_2$ to $T_3$.

### Q3. What is cold-junction compensation? Why is it required?  `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Cold-junction compensation (CJC) is the technique of mathematically or electronically correcting the thermocouple EMF so that the reference-junction temperature is treated as **0 °C** (or any other fixed temperature), regardless of the actual physical terminal-block temperature.
- It is required because **standard reference tables assume a 0 °C reference**, but in practice the cold junction sits at ambient (≈ 25 °C). Without CJC, errors of several degrees are introduced.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

> **Q A(a). With a neat block diagram, explain the procedure to calibrate a K-type thermocouple using the comparison method. List the apparatus required. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Aim (½ mark):** To determine the relationship between the thermoelectric EMF and the temperature of a K-type thermocouple by comparison with a standard mercury-in-glass thermometer and to plot the calibration curve.

**2. Apparatus Required (1 mark):**
- K-type thermocouple (test specimen).
- Mercury thermometer (0–110 °C, ±0.1 °C).
- Thermostatically controlled water bath with stirrer.
- Vacuum flask with crushed ice + distilled water (0 °C reference).
- Digital millivolt meter (resolution 1 µV, input impedance ≥ 10 MΩ).
- K-type compensating cable, copper leads, beakers, stopwatch.

**3. Block Diagram (2 marks):** *(As shown in SECTION 4, Mermaid diagram.)*

**4. Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Prepare the ice bath — fill the dewar with finely crushed ice, add distilled water, stir, and immerse the cold-junction end of the thermocouple to a depth of at least **10 cm** along with a check thermometer.
2. Set the water bath to the first calibration temperature (typically 30 °C). Switch on the heater and stirrer.
3. Immerse the *measuring junction* of the thermocouple and the bulb of the mercury thermometer together to a depth of at least **5 cm** below the water surface.
4. Wait **5–10 minutes** for thermal equilibrium (the mercury column and the millivolt reading must both stabilise).
5. Note the **standard temperature $T_s$** from the mercury thermometer and the corresponding **thermocouple EMF $E$** from the DMM.
6. Raise the bath temperature in steps of **10 °C** up to 100 °C, repeating steps 4–5 at each step.
7. Tabulate $(T_s, E)$ and plot the calibration curve $E$ (mV) vs $T$ (°C).
8. Compare the measured EMF with the **IEC 60584-1 standard table** value $E_{std}$ and compute the per-point correction $\Delta T$.

**5. Precautions (1 mark):**
- Cold-junction depth in ice bath must be sufficient — at least 10 cm.
- Stirrer must be on continuously to avoid temperature gradients.
- Avoid contamination of the ice bath (use distilled water).
- Do not bend the thermocouple wires sharply at the measuring junction.
- Allow adequate settling time before recording each reading.

**6. Result (½ mark):** The plot of $E$ vs $T$ is essentially linear with an average sensitivity of about **41 µV/°C** for a K-type thermocouple in the 0–100 °C range, matching the IEC 60584-1 reference values within the standard tolerance class.

---

> **Q A(b). A K-type thermocouple with cold junction in an ice bath is used to measure a hot-junction temperature of 250 °C. The millivolt meter reads 10.150 mV. The standard table value for K-type at 250 °C (cold junction at 0 °C) is 10.153 mV. Calculate (i) the EMF error, (ii) the temperature error, and (iii) the percentage error. The Seebeck coefficient at 250 °C is 42.5 µV/°C. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $E_{measured} = 10.150$ mV
- $E_{standard} = 10.153$ mV at $T = 250$ °C
- $S_{AB}(250^\circ C) = 42.5 \,\mu V/°C = 0.0425$ mV/°C

**Part (i) — EMF error (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta E \;=\; E_{measured} - E_{standard} \;=\; 10.150 - 10.153 \;=\; -0.003 \text{ mV} \;=\; -3 \,\mu V
$$

**Part (ii) — Temperature error (2.5 marks):**

$$
\Delta T \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta E}{S_{AB}} \;=\; \dfrac{-0.003 \text{ mV}}{0.0425 \text{ mV/°C}}
$$

$$
\Delta T \;\approx\; -0.0706 \text{ °C} \;\approx\; -0.07 \text{ °C}
$$

The negative sign indicates the thermocouple reads *lower* than the true temperature by about 0.07 °C.

**Part (iii) — Percentage error (1.5 marks):**

$$
\% \text{ Error} \;=\; \dfrac{\vert T_{indicated} - T_{actual} \vert}{T_{actual}} \times 100
$$

The *indicated* temperature is obtained by looking up $E_{measured}$ in the K-type table. Since $E_{measured}$ is *less* than $E_{standard}$, the indicated temperature will be *less* than 250 °C. Using the linear approximation:

$$
T_{indicated} \;\approx\; 250 + \Delta T \;=\; 250 - 0.07 \;=\; 249.93 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\% \text{ Error} \;=\; \dfrac{\vert 249.93 - 250 \vert}{250} \times 100 \;=\; \dfrac{0.07}{250} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Error} \;\approx\; 0.028 \,\%
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The K-type thermocouple is well within its standard accuracy class; the error is negligible for industrial use.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** (Alternative Choice)

> **Q B(a). With the help of a functional block diagram, describe the method of cold-junction compensation of a thermocouple. Explain the role of the law of intermediate temperatures. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):** Standard reference tables (IEC 60584) tabulate thermocouple EMFs assuming the reference (cold) junction is at exactly **0 °C**. In a real instrument, the cold junction is at the terminal block, which is at ambient temperature (typically 25–40 °C). Cold-junction compensation (CJC) is the technique used to *re-reference* the measured EMF back to 0 °C so that the standard table can be used.

**2. Functional Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
MEASURING JUNCTION (T_hot)
        |
        |  Thermocouple wires
        v
+--------------------------+
| COLD JUNCTION (T_cj)      |  <-- At terminal block; temperature
|     sensed by internal    |      measured by extra sensor
|     silicon diode / PT100 |
+--------------------------+
        |
        |  Copper leads (intermediate metal)
        v
+--------------------------+
| AMPLIFIER + ADC          |
+--------------------------+
        |
        v
+--------------------------+
| MICROCONTROLLER / DSP    |  <-- Looks up E(T_cj, 0°C) from
| (Applies Law of IT)      |      stored table; adds it to E_meas
+--------------------------+
        |
        v
+--------------------------+
| DISPLAY : T_hot (°C)     |
+--------------------------+
```

**3. Mathematical Procedure (2.5 marks):**

Using the **Law of Intermediate Temperatures**:

$$
E_{AB}\bigl(T_{hot},\,0^\circ C\bigr) \;=\; E_{measured} \;+\; E_{AB}\bigl(T_{cj},\,0^\circ C\bigr)
$$

where:
- $E_{measured} = E_{AB}(T_{hot},\,T_{cj})$ — the voltage read by the ADC.
- $E_{AB}(T_{cj},\,0^\circ C)$ — the *compensating* EMF, looked up from the standard table using the *terminal-block temperature* $T_{cj}$ measured by the internal sensor.

The sum is then looked up in the same table to obtain the *true* $T_{hot}$.

**4. Role of the Law of Intermediate Temperatures (1 mark):**
This law is the **mathematical foundation** of CJC — it guarantees that the EMF for a wide temperature span can be reconstructed by *adding* the EMFs of two narrower spans whose union equals the original span. Without this law, CJC would be impossible.

**5. Conclusion (½ mark):** CJC is essential for accurate digital thermocouple instrumentation and is implemented almost universally in modern indicators.

---

> **Q B(b). A K-type thermocouple is used with its reference junction at 30 °C (not 0 °C). The voltmeter reads 7.520 mV. The IEC 60584-1 K-type table gives $E_K(30^\circ C,\,0^\circ C) = 1.203$ mV. Find (i) the corrected EMF as referenced to 0 °C, and (ii) the corresponding hot-junction temperature. (Use standard K-type table values.) (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Measured EMF: $E_m = 7.520$ mV (this is $E_K(T_{hot},\,30^\circ C)$)
- Compensating EMF: $E_K(30^\circ C,\,0^\circ C) = 1.203$ mV
- Reference table: **IEC 60584-1 K-type**.

**Part (i) — Corrected EMF (3 marks):**

By the **Law of Intermediate Temperatures**:

$$
E_K(T_{hot},\,0^\circ C) \;=\; E_K(T_{hot},\,30^\circ C) \;+\; E_K(30^\circ C,\,0^\circ C)
$$

$$
E_{corrected} \;=\; 7.520 + 1.203 \;=\; 8.723 \text{ mV}
$$

**Part (ii) — Hot-junction temperature (4 marks):**

Look up $E = 8.723$ mV in the K-type table (cold junction at 0 °C).

From the IEC 60584-1 K-type table:

| EMF (mV) | Temperature (°C) |
|----------|------------------|
| 8.561 | 200 |
| 8.685 | 203 |
| 8.809 | 206 |
| 8.933 | 209 |

Linear interpolation between 8.685 mV (203 °C) and 8.809 mV (206 °C):

$$
\Delta E_{table} \;=\; 8.809 - 8.685 \;=\; 0.124 \text{ mV per 3 °C}
$$

$$
\Delta E_{above} \;=\; 8.723 - 8.685 \;=\; 0.038 \text{ mV}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{fraction} \;=\; \dfrac{0.038}{0.124} \times 3 \;=\; 0.919 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
T_{hot} \;=\; 203 + 0.919 \;\approx\; 203.92 \text{ °C} \;\approx\; 204 \text{ °C}
$$

**Conclusion:** Without CJC, the instrument would have looked up 7.520 mV in the table and reported ≈ 175 °C — an error of nearly 30 °C. CJC restores the correct reading to within interpolation precision.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on this experiment:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the Seebeck Effect explicitly** in the *Theory* section — it is the *core* principle and is worth at least 1 mark by itself.
> 2. **Omitting the Law of Intermediate Metals** when describing the use of copper leads. Examiners specifically look for this law to justify connecting a voltmeter without altering the EMF.
> 3. **No mention of cold-junction compensation** when reporting a digital readout value. If your instrument does *not* have an ice bath at the cold junction, you *must* state how CJC is performed (manual, software, hardware).
> 4. **Insufficient immersion depth** — a depth of < 5 cm in the bath causes *stem-correction* errors. Always state the depth.
> 5. **No repeatability check** — a calibration is incomplete without at least one *repeat* reading at a chosen temperature to demonstrate the static repeatability of the thermocouple-instrument pair.
> 6. **Plotting with non-ideal axes** — the EMF axis must be in **mV** (not V), and the temperature axis in **°C** (not K). The plot must have a *title*, *labelled axes*, and *units*.
> 7. **Not computing the percentage error** — for a 7-mark problem on calibration, examiners expect at least a numerical sensitivity, an EMF error, and a percentage error. Skipping any of these costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Thermocouple = two dissimilar metals + temperature difference → EMF (Seebeck Effect).**
- **Calibration** establishes the E vs T relationship for a *specific* thermocouple using a *traceable* standard.
- **Three Laws of Thermoelectric Circuits** are guaranteed in KTU theory questions:
  - Law of Homogeneous Materials
  - Law of Intermediate Metals (justifies copper leads, solder joints)
  - Law of Intermediate Temperatures (foundation of CJC)
- **Seebeck integral:** $E_{AB} = \int S_{AB}(T)\, dT$. For small ranges, $E_{AB} \approx S_{AB}\,\Delta T$.
- **K-type sensitivity** ≈ **41 µV/°C** in the 0–100 °C range. **T-type** ≈ 40 µV/°C. **J-type** ≈ 50–55 µV/°C.
- **Cold-junction compensation formula:** $E_{AB}(T_{hot}, 0) = E_{measured} + E_{AB}(T_{cj}, 0)$.
- **Reference junction in lab** is maintained at **0 °C** using an **ice bath** (crushed ice + distilled water in a dewar).
- **Calibration curve** is a plot of **EMF (mV) on y-axis** vs **Temperature (°C) on x-axis** — always labelled and titled.
- **Correction per point:** $\Delta T = \Delta E \,/\, S_{AB}(T)$.
- **Apparatus checklist:** thermocouple + reference thermometer + stirred bath + ice bath + DMM (≥ 10 MΩ input) + compensating cable.
- **Standard reference documents:** **IEC 60584-1** (EMF tables), **IEC 60584-2** (tolerance classes), **NIST ITS-90** (thermodynamic basis).
- **Repeatability** must be reported; **precautions** must include immersion depth, stirrer, ice-bath purity, and thermal equilibrium time.
- **Industrial relevance:** Used in furnaces, gas turbines, HVAC, nuclear reactors, food/pharma process validation.
- **Common KTU mistakes:** forgetting the Seebeck effect, omitting CJC, wrong plot units, no repeatability check, no error percentage.

topics.title: Measurement of Level Using Differential Pressure Transmitter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL308 - INSTRUMENTATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Level Using Differential Pressure Transmitter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differential Pressure Transmitter (DPT) based Level Measurement** is an indirect method of determining the liquid level inside a closed or open vessel by sensing the **hydrostatic pressure** exerted by the liquid column above a reference point. The DPT measures the difference in pressure between two points separated vertically in the process fluid (High Pressure side, HP) and a reference column (Low Pressure side, LP), and converts this differential pressure ($\Delta P$) into a standardized analog signal (**4–20 mA DC**) proportional to the liquid level.

The governing physical relationship is given by the fundamental hydrostatic equation:

$$\Delta P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

where $\rho$ is the density of the liquid, $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**), and $h$ is the height of the liquid column.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Breathing Straw" Principle:**
> Imagine you insert a long straw into a glass of water and suck gently. The taller the water in the glass, the harder you have to suck. That "suck force" you feel at your lips is exactly the *hydrostatic pressure* the water column exerts. A Differential Pressure Transmitter works on the same idea: instead of your mouth, it has a **silicone diaphragm** that flexes inward in proportion to the pressure pushing up from the liquid below. By measuring how much it flexes, the instrument "knows" how tall the liquid column is — without ever touching the liquid surface.

### 1.2.1 Real-World Significance

In industries such as **petrochemical refineries, power plants, water treatment facilities, and pharmaceutical manufacturing**, continuous level monitoring is non-negotiable for:
- Process safety (preventing tank overflow or dry-run of pumps)
- Inventory management
- Custody transfer of liquids
- Closed-loop control of feeding pumps

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **HP Side (High Pressure)** | Lower tap of the DPT — connected near the bottom of the tank |
| **LP Side (Low Pressure)** | Upper tap — connected above the liquid or to a reference (wet/dry leg) |
| **Wet Leg** | LP impulse tube filled with the same process liquid (condensate pot arrangement) |
| **Dry Leg** | LP impulse tube filled with air/inert gas (used in open tanks) |
| **Suppression (Zero Elevation)** | DPT measures level when liquid is *above* the HP tap |
| **Elevation (Zero Suppression)** | DPT measures level when liquid is *below* the HP tap |
| **3-Valve Manifold** | Block-and-bleed valve assembly used for isolation and zero calibration |
| **LRV / URV** | Lower Range Value / Upper Range Value (calibration endpoints) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students are expected to identify the components of a DPT-based level measurement loop, perform **calibration (zero & span)**, compute **suppression/elevation offsets**, and plot the **input–output characteristic curve** in the lab record.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The Differential Pressure Transmitter is essentially a **force-balance device**. Here is the step-by-step logic:

1. **Hydrostatic Force Generation:** The liquid column of height $h$ above the lower tap exerts pressure on the **HP diaphragm** of the DPT. The reference side (LP) sees either atmospheric pressure (open tank) or a wet-leg reference pressure (closed tank).

2. **Diaphragm Deflection:** The pressure difference deflects a sensing diaphragm. The deflection is minute (in micrometers) but is converted into an electrical signal via one of three transducer mechanisms:
   - **Capacitive sensing** (most common in modern DPTs like Rosemount 3051)
   - **Piezoelectric sensing**
   - **Strain-gauge (piezoresistive) bridge**

3. **Signal Conditioning:** The millivolt-level signal is amplified, linearized, and converted to a **4–20 mA current loop** by the transmitter electronics.

4. **Current Loop Transmission:** The 4 mA represents the **Lower Range Value (LRV)**, and 20 mA represents the **Upper Range Value (URV)**. The 4 mA "live zero" allows detection of cable breaks (a reading of 0 mA indicates an open loop fault).

5. **HART Protocol (Optional):** Many modern DPTs superimpose a digital **Highway Addressable Remote Transducer (HART)** signal on the 4–20 mA loop, enabling remote calibration via **375/475 Field Communicator**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The DPT does **not** measure level directly. It measures a *pressure differential* and infers level using $\Delta P = \rho g h$. This means any change in **liquid density** (e.g., due to temperature or composition) introduces a measurement error unless density compensation is applied.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure due to liquid column | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $h = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho \cdot g}$ | Level computed from differential pressure | m |
| 3 | $\Delta P_{\max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h_{\max}$ | Maximum differential pressure (used to set URV) | Pa |
| 4 | $I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{(h - h_{\min})}{(h_{\max} - h_{\min})}$ | Linear 4–20 mA conversion for level | mA |
| 5 | $I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{(\Delta P - \Delta P_{\min})}{(\Delta P_{\max} - \Delta P_{\min})}$ | 4–20 mA conversion in terms of pressure | mA |
| 6 | $\Delta P_{\text{elev}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H_e$ | Elevation offset (when HP tap is below tank bottom) | Pa |
| 7 | $\Delta P_{\text{supp}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H_s$ | Suppression offset (when HP tap is above tank bottom) | Pa |
| 8 | $\% \text{ Span} = \dfrac{(\text{Measured} - \text{LRV})}{(\text{URV} - \text{LRV})} \times 100$ | Percentage of calibrated span | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to Memorize:**
> - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Density of water at 4 °C: $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 100 \text{ kPa}$
> - $1 \text{ mH}_2\text{O} = 9.81 \text{ kPa}$ (approx. 9.79 kPa at standard conditions)

## 2.3 Configurations of DPT for Level Measurement

### 2.3.1 Open Tank — Dry Leg Configuration
The LP tap is vented to the atmosphere. $\Delta P$ is purely the hydrostatic head.

$$\Delta P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

### 2.3.2 Closed Tank — Wet Leg Configuration
The LP tap is connected via an impulse tube filled with a **condensate** (e.g., water for hydrocarbon service). The wet-leg column exerts a *constant* pressure on the LP side that opposes the HP side pressure.

$$\Delta P = \rho_{\text{process}} \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_{\text{condensate}} \cdot g \cdot h_{\text{wet leg}}$$

### 2.3.3 Suppressed Zero (Suppression)
Used when the **HP tap is mounted above the tank bottom** by a distance $H_s$. The transmitter must "ignore" the static pressure corresponding to $H_s$.

$$\Delta P_{\text{calibrated}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot (h - H_s)$$

The LRV is set to a *negative* equivalent suppression value during calibration.

### 2.3.4 Elevated Zero (Elevation)
Used when the **HP tap is mounted below the tank bottom** (e.g., in a buried sump). The transmitter must "cancel" the static head of the liquid that would otherwise push the reading above zero at empty condition.

$$\Delta P_{\text{calibrated}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot (h + H_e)$$

The LRV is set to a *positive* elevation offset.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| Oil & Gas | Hydrocarbon storage tank level, interface detection |
| Power Plants | Boiler drum level (critical for safety) |
| Pharma | Reactor vessel level for batch processing |
| Water Treatment | Clarifier, filter bed, and chemical dosing tanks |
| Food & Beverage | Mixing tank level in dairy and brewing |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Current as a Function of Liquid Level

**Problem Setup:** A DPT is mounted to measure the level of water in an open tank. The HP tap is at the bottom of the tank (no suppression/elevation). The calibration range is 0 to 5 m. Derive the expression for output current $I_{\text{out}}$ as a function of water level $h$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the input variable:**
The input to the DPT is the hydrostatic pressure of the water column.

$$\Delta P = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h$$

Substituting the standard values $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$$\Delta P = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot h = 9810 \cdot h \quad \text{(in Pa, with } h \text{ in meters)}$$

**Step 2 — Define the calibration endpoints:**

For $h = 0$ m (empty tank):
$$\Delta P_{\min} = 9810 \cdot 0 = 0 \text{ Pa}$$
This corresponds to the LRV → $I_{\text{out}} = 4$ mA.

For $h = 5$ m (full tank):
$$\Delta P_{\max} = 9810 \cdot 5 = 49050 \text{ Pa} = 49.05 \text{ kPa}$$
This corresponds to the URV → $I_{\text{out}} = 20$ mA.

**Step 3 — Apply the linear transfer function of the current loop:**

The 4–20 mA loop is *linear* in the measured variable (pressure or level). The general form is:

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + (20 - 4) \cdot \dfrac{(\Delta P - \Delta P_{\min})}{(\Delta P_{\max} - \Delta P_{\min})}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the calibration values:**

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{(\Delta P - 0)}{(49050 - 0)} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{49050}$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of level $h$ (substitute $\Delta P = 9810 h$):**

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{9810 \cdot h}{49050} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{h}{5}$$

**Step 6 — Final simplified expression:**

$$\boxed{I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 3.2 \cdot h \quad \text{(mA, with } h \text{ in meters)}}$$

**Verification:**
- At $h = 0$ m: $I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 3.2(0) = 4$ mA ✓
- At $h = 5$ m: $I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 3.2(5) = 4 + 16 = 20$ mA ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Suppressed Zero Calibration

**Problem Setup:** The HP tap of the DPT is mounted **0.8 m above the tank bottom** in an open tank containing a liquid of density $\rho = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The level range to be measured is **0 to 4 m** (measured from the tank bottom). Find the LRV and URV in terms of $\Delta P$ and the output current equation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Understand suppression:**
When the HP tap is at height $H_s = 0.8$ m above the tank bottom, the transmitter senses zero hydrostatic pressure until the liquid level rises to $h = 0.8$ m. The "suppressed" portion of the head must be zeroed out.

**Step 2 — Compute the suppression pressure offset:**

$$\Delta P_{\text{supp}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H_s = 850 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.8$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$\Delta P_{\text{supp}} = 850 \cdot 9.81 = 8338.5$$
$$8338.5 \cdot 0.8 = 6670.8 \text{ Pa} \approx 6.67 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the differential pressure at minimum level ($h = 0$ m):**

The HP tap is at 0.8 m. The actual liquid level is 0 m. The liquid does not reach the HP tap, so:

$$\Delta P_{\min} = 0 - 6670.8 = -6670.8 \text{ Pa} = -6.67 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the differential pressure at maximum level ($h = 4$ m):**

$$\Delta P = 850 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (4 - 0.8) = 850 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 3.2$$

$$850 \cdot 9.81 = 8338.5$$
$$8338.5 \cdot 3.2 = 26683.2 \text{ Pa} \approx 26.68 \text{ kPa}$$

So $\Delta P_{\max} = +26.68 \text{ kPa}$.

**Step 5 — Establish the LRV and URV:**

| Calibration Point | Level $h$ | $\Delta P$ | $I_{\text{out}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| LRV | 0 m | $-6.67$ kPa | 4 mA |
| URV | 4 m | $+26.68$ kPa | 20 mA |

**Step 6 — Derive the output current equation:**

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P - \Delta P_{\min}}{\Delta P_{\max} - \Delta P_{\min}}$$

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P - (-6670.8)}{26683.2 - (-6670.8)}$$

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P + 6670.8}{33354.0}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $\Delta P = 850 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (h - 0.8) = 8338.5 (h - 0.8)$:**

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{8338.5(h - 0.8) + 6670.8}{33354.0}$$

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{8338.5 h - 6670.8 + 6670.8}{33354.0}$$

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{8338.5 \cdot h}{33354.0} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{h}{4}$$

**Step 8 — Final simplified expression:**

$$\boxed{I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 4 \cdot h \quad \text{(mA, with } h \text{ in meters)}}$$

**Verification:**
- At $h = 0$: $I_{\text{out}} = 4$ mA ✓
- At $h = 4$: $I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 4(4) = 20$ mA ✓
- The slope has increased from 3.2 mA/m to 4 mA/m, which is consistent with the reduced effective span.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wet Leg Configuration in a Closed Tank

**Problem Setup:** A closed tank contains oil of density $\rho_{\text{oil}} = 780 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The wet-leg (condensate) is water with $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The wet-leg height is $h_{\text{wet}} = 1.2$ m. The level range is 0 to 3 m. Derive the output current equation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply the wet-leg pressure equation:**

$$\Delta P = \rho_{\text{oil}} \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h_{\text{wet}}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the wet-leg offset (constant for a given installation):**

$$\Delta P_{\text{wet}} = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1.2 = 11772 \text{ Pa} = 11.77 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\Delta P$ at $h = 0$ m (empty):**

$$\Delta P_{\min} = 780 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0 - 11772 = -11772 \text{ Pa} = -11.77 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\Delta P$ at $h = 3$ m (full):**

$$\Delta P = 780 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 3 - 11772 = 22955.4 - 11772 = 11183.4 \text{ Pa} = 11.18 \text{ kPa}$$

So $\Delta P_{\max} = +11.18$ kPa.

**Step 5 — Compute the calibrated span:**

$$\text{Span} = \Delta P_{\max} - \Delta P_{\min} = 11.18 - (-11.77) = 22.95 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 6 — Build the output current equation:**

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P - (-11772)}{22954.8} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P + 11772}{22954.8}$$

**Step 7 — Final expression in terms of $h$:**

$$\boxed{I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 5.575 \cdot h \quad \text{(mA, with } h \text{ in meters)}}$$

**Verification:**
- At $h = 0$: $I_{\text{out}} = 4$ mA ✓
- At $h = 3$: $I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 5.575(3) = 4 + 16.73 \approx 20$ mA ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical Exam Numerical Patterns

| Scenario | Given | Find | Key Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Open tank, simple | $\rho$, $h_{\max}$ | $I_{\text{out}}$ at intermediate $h$ | $I = 4 + \frac{16 \rho g h}{\rho g h_{\max}}$ |
| Open tank, suppressed | $H_s$, $\rho$, $h_{\max}$ | LRV, URV, slope | LRV $= -\rho g H_s$ |
| Open tank, elevated | $H_e$, $\rho$, $h_{\max}$ | LRV, URV, slope | LRV $= +\rho g H_e$ |
| Closed tank, wet leg | $\rho_{\text{oil}}$, $\rho_{\text{water}}$, $h_{\text{wet}}$ | Calibrated span | Span $= (\rho_{\text{oil}} - \rho_w) g h$ |
| Reverse-acting output | 4 mA at full, 20 mA at empty | Reverse slope | $I = 20 - 16 \cdot \frac{h}{h_{\max}}$ |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Differential Pressure Transmitter. Mention its role in level measurement. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Differential Pressure Transmitter (DPT)** is a process instrumentation device that measures the difference between two pressure inputs — the **High Pressure (HP)** and **Low Pressure (LP)** sides — and converts this differential into a standardized **4–20 mA DC** analog output signal.

In **level measurement**, the DPT exploits the **hydrostatic principle** ($\Delta P = \rho g h$). The HP tap is connected to the bottom of the tank, where the liquid column exerts maximum pressure. The LP tap is connected to either the atmosphere (open tank) or a reference column (closed tank, wet leg). The differential pressure is directly proportional to the liquid level $h$, enabling continuous, real-time level monitoring without contact with the liquid surface.

---

### Question 2: What is a wet leg? Why is it used in closed tank level measurement? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **wet leg** is the LP-side impulse tube in a closed-tank DPT installation, which is intentionally filled with a **condensable reference liquid** (typically water for hydrocarbon service). A **condensate pot** is installed at the top of the wet leg to maintain the liquid seal.

**Purpose:**
1. The wet leg provides a **constant, known reference pressure** on the LP side of the DPT, which does not change with the process level.
2. It allows the DPT to measure **only the variable hydrostatic head** of the process liquid, improving accuracy.
3. It protects the LP side of the DPT from **corrosive vapors** and high temperatures by buffering them with the condensate.
4. It enables measurement in **pressurized closed tanks**, where the LP side cannot be vented to atmosphere.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain with a neat sketch the working of a Differential Pressure Transmitter (DPT) for level measurement in an open tank. Also derive the relationship between output current and liquid level. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (Sketch Description):**

The open-tank DPT level measurement setup consists of:
- A **process tank** containing the liquid whose level is to be measured.
- The **HP tap** of the DPT is connected via an impulse tube to a point **near the bottom of the tank** (typically slightly above the bottom to avoid sludge).
- The **LP tap** is **vented to atmosphere** (dry leg configuration).
- A **3-valve manifold** is installed between the impulse tubes and the DPT for isolation and zeroing.
- The DPT outputs a **4–20 mA** signal to a **PLC, DCS, or indicator**.

**Working:**
1. The hydrostatic pressure exerted by the liquid column of height $h$ acts on the HP diaphragm.
2. Since the LP side is at atmospheric pressure, the differential pressure is:

$$\Delta P = P_{\text{HP}} - P_{\text{LP}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h - 0 = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

3. The diaphragm deflects, and a capacitive/piezoresistive sensor converts this deflection into a voltage.
4. The transmitter electronics linearize and scale the signal to a **4–20 mA** output.

**Derivation:**

Let the calibration range be $h_{\min}$ to $h_{\max}$, corresponding to $\Delta P_{\min}$ to $\Delta P_{\max}$.

By the linear 4–20 mA transfer function:

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P - \Delta P_{\min}}{\Delta P_{\max} - \Delta P_{\min}}$$

For an open tank with HP at the bottom:
- $\Delta P_{\min} = \rho g h_{\min}$
- $\Delta P_{\max} = \rho g h_{\max}$

Substituting:

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{\rho g h - \rho g h_{\min}}{\rho g h_{\max} - \rho g h_{\min}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{h - h_{\min}}{h_{\max} - h_{\min}}$$

If $h_{\min} = 0$:

$$\boxed{I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{h}{h_{\max}}}$$

**Conclusion:** The output current is **linearly proportional** to the liquid level. A 12 mA reading (mid-scale) corresponds to 50% of full level.

---

#### (b) A DPT is installed in a closed tank containing a liquid of specific gravity 0.85. The wet leg is filled with water and has a height of 1.5 m. The level measurement range is 0–5 m. Calculate: **(i) The differential pressure at 0% and 100% level. (ii) The output current when the level is at 3 m. (iii) The calibrated span in kPa.** **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Specific gravity of process liquid: $SG = 0.85 \Rightarrow \rho_{\text{oil}} = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Density of water (wet leg): $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Wet leg height: $h_{\text{wet}} = 1.5$ m
- Level range: $0$ to $5$ m
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Formula for wet leg configuration:**

$$\Delta P = \rho_{\text{oil}} \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h_{\text{wet}}$$

**(i) Differential pressure at 0% level ($h = 0$ m):**

$$\Delta P_{\min} = 850 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0 - 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1.5$$

$$\Delta P_{\min} = 0 - 14715 = -14715 \text{ Pa} = -14.715 \text{ kPa}$$

**Differential pressure at 100% level ($h = 5$ m):**

$$\Delta P_{\max} = 850 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 5 - 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1.5$$

$$\Delta P_{\max} = 41692.5 - 14715 = 26977.5 \text{ Pa} = +26.978 \text{ kPa}$$

**(ii) Output current at $h = 3$ m:**

$$\Delta P \text{ at } h = 3 \text{ m} = 850 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 3 - 14715 = 25015.5 - 14715 = 10300.5 \text{ Pa}$$

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P - \Delta P_{\min}}{\Delta P_{\max} - \Delta P_{\min}}$$

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{10300.5 - (-14715)}{26977.5 - (-14715)} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{25015.5}{41692.5}$$

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot 0.6 = 4 + 9.6 = 13.6 \text{ mA}$$

**(iii) Calibrated span:**

$$\text{Span} = \Delta P_{\max} - \Delta P_{\min} = 26.978 - (-14.715) = 41.693 \text{ kPa}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $\Delta P_{\min}$ (at 0% level) | $-14.72$ kPa |
| $\Delta P_{\max}$ (at 100% level) | $+26.98$ kPa |
| Calibrated span | $41.69$ kPa |
| Output current at $h = 3$ m | $13.6$ mA |
